ROSMAP = Religious Orders Study/Memory and Aging Project; ADNI = Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative.     

9. AI in Target Identification, Validation, and Drug Repurposing for Neurodegeneration
The identification of a valid therapeutic target—a biological molecule whose modulation will produce a disease-relevant pharmacological effect without unacceptable toxicity—is the critical first step of the drug discovery pipeline and a major source of late-stage attrition. Historically, target identification relied on genetic association studies, phenotypic observations, and hypothesis-driven experimental biology. Artificial intelligence is transforming this process by enabling the systematic, data-driven prioritization of targets across the entire molecular landscape of a disease.
Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of genetic risk loci for AD, PD, and other neurodegenerative diseases, but translating statistical genetic associations into actionable therapeutic hypotheses requires sophisticated computational prioritization. Bayesian colocalization analyses, Mendelian randomization, and fine-mapping algorithms disentangle causal variants from linkage disequilibrium, while machine learning models trained on transcriptomics data predict the functional consequences of risk variants on gene expression (eQTL effects), splicing (sQTL), and protein abundance (pQTL). The integration of GWAS summary statistics with tissue-specific expression data through methods such as S-PrediXcan and FUSION enables the prioritization of genetically regulated genes as drug targets, with those showing evidence of causal involvement rather than mere statistical association receiving the highest priority scores.
Knowledge graph-based target identification leverages the rich semantic connectivity of biomedical knowledge to identify targets at the intersection of disease biology and druggable protein families. Graph machine learning algorithms—including node embedding methods (node2vec, DeepWalk), relational graph convolutional networks (R-GCN), and knowledge graph completion models (TransE, RotatE)—learn vector representations of biological entities that reflect their functional relationships. Applied to Hetionet or similar disease-specific knowledge graphs, these models predict novel disease-gene associations with high specificity by identifying gene nodes that are topologically proximate to established disease mechanisms. In the context of neurodegeneration, such approaches have highlighted lysosomal dysfunction, neuroinflammation signaling (particularly TREM2-APOE-TYROBP axis), mitochondrial quality control, and RNA splicing regulation as computationally enriched target spaces.
Drug repurposing—the identification of new therapeutic applications for approved or investigational drugs—offers a compelling risk-reduction strategy given the established safety profiles and pharmacokinetic characterization of the repurposed candidates. AI-driven repurposing approaches span several methodological classes. Signature-based methods (e.g., using the LINCS L1000 perturbational transcriptomics database) identify drugs whose gene expression signatures are inversely correlated with disease expression profiles, as exemplified by the CMap (Connectivity Map) approach. Network-based repurposing exploits the principle that drugs with targets proximal to disease genes in the interactome network are more likely to modulate disease-relevant biology. Deep learning models trained on multi-source drug-disease interaction data can predict repurposing opportunities with greater accuracy than single-data-type approaches.
Several compelling AI-driven repurposing predictions have been validated experimentally or advanced to clinical trials in the neurodegeneration space. Fang et al. (2022) used a network medicine framework to identify sildenafil (a PDE5 inhibitor) as a candidate AD drug based on its proximal targeting of tau phosphorylation and amyloid processing networks—a prediction subsequently supported by retrospective analysis of insurance claims data showing reduced AD incidence in sildenafil users. AI-based analyses of UK Biobank data have identified metformin, losartan, and several repurposed anti-inflammatory agents as associated with reduced AD risk, generating testable repurposing hypotheses that are now being pursued in clinical trials.
Table 8 summarizes key AI platforms used in drug discovery, and Figure 7 (to be created by authors) should illustrate an AI-driven target identification workflow for Alzheimer's disease: starting from multi-omics data inputs (GWAS, transcriptomics, proteomics), flowing through a knowledge graph integration layer, processed by a GNN-based target prioritization model, and outputting a ranked list of candidate targets annotated by druggability score, genetic evidence tier, and tissue expression profile. Include a sidebar showing the network proximity concept—a bipartite graph with drug targets on one side and AD seed genes on the other, with proximity scores visualized by edge thickness.

Table 8. AI Platforms and Software Tools for Drug Discovery

10. Explainable AI and Federated Learning: Toward Trustworthy Drug Discovery
The increasing deployment of deep learning models in high-stakes drug discovery decisions has intensified scrutiny of model interpretability and trustworthiness. Regulatory agencies, including the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Medicines Agency (EMA), have issued guidance emphasizing the importance of model transparency, bias assessment, and audit trails for AI systems used in pharmaceutical development. The field of explainable AI (XAI) addresses these imperatives by developing methods that illuminate the reasoning processes of complex models, enabling scientists to verify that predictions are grounded in chemically meaningful features rather than spurious correlations.
Model-agnostic XAI methods—applicable regardless of the underlying model architecture—include LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), which approximates a complex model locally with a simpler interpretable surrogate, and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), which attributes individual model output values to each input feature based on game-theoretic Shapley values. In molecular property prediction, SHAP has been applied to identify the specific atoms, bonds, and substructural fragments most responsible for predicted ADMET properties, enabling medicinal chemists to design targeted structural modifications that improve a desired property while minimizing collateral impacts. AttentiveFP's graph attention mechanism provides intrinsic interpretability by directly assigning learned importance weights to atoms and bonds, producing visualizable attribution maps that highlight pharmacophoric features driving activity predictions.
For protein structure-based models, gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) and integrated gradients methods can highlight the residues in a binding pocket that most strongly influence predicted binding affinity or selectivity, directly guiding structure-based optimization. In biomedical NLP, attention visualization tools applied to BioBERT and PubMedBERT models reveal which token pairs in a text the model uses to support relation extraction predictions, enabling human experts to validate or correct the model's semantic reasoning. The development of XAI-enhanced dashboards for drug discovery workflows—integrating predictions, uncertainty estimates, and attribution maps in a single decision-support interface—is an active area of tool development by both academic groups and pharmaceutical informatics teams.
Federated learning (FL) addresses a complementary challenge in AI-driven drug discovery: the need to train models on large, diverse datasets while respecting data privacy, proprietary ownership, and regulatory constraints that preclude the centralization of sensitive patient data or confidential assay results. In the federated learning paradigm, a global model is trained collaboratively across multiple institutions or organizations, with each participant computing gradients on their local data and contributing only aggregated parameter updates—never raw data—to the central model server. This architecture enables the pooling of statistical power across institutions without the privacy, security, and IP risks of data centralization.
The MELLODDY consortium (Machine Learning Ledger Orchestration for Drug Discovery), a landmark industry-academia collaboration spanning ten major pharmaceutical companies, demonstrated the feasibility and substantial performance gains of privacy-preserving federated multi-task learning for bioactivity prediction. By training a shared deep neural network across the pooled compound-activity data of all consortium members—without any company disclosing proprietary structures or assay data to competitors—the MELLODDY model substantially outperformed models trained on individual company datasets alone, particularly for orphan targets with limited in-house data coverage. This proof-of-concept has catalyzed ongoing federated learning initiatives in neurodegenerative disease research, where multi-center patient cohorts are essential for capturing the full biological heterogeneity of these conditions.
Figure 8 (to be created by authors) should provide a side-by-side visual comparison of centralized versus federated learning architectures for drug discovery: the left panel should show the traditional centralized approach (data from multiple hospitals/pharma companies flowing to a single central server, training a unified model), while the right panel depicts the federated approach (each institution trains locally, only model parameters flow to the central aggregator, data remains siloed). Annotate privacy-preserving mechanisms (differential privacy noise injection, secure aggregation) and show the global model update cycle. Use institution icons specific to pharmaceutical and clinical settings.
11. Future Challenges and Research Frontiers
Despite the transformative advances documented in this chapter, several fundamental challenges constrain the full realization of AI's potential in drug discovery, particularly for neurodegenerative diseases. Addressing these challenges will require concerted effort from the computational, experimental, and clinical research communities, as well as thoughtful engagement with regulatory bodies, ethics experts, and patient advocacy organizations.
Data scarcity, imbalance, and reproducibility. The performance of supervised machine learning models scales with the quantity and quality of labeled training data—a resource that remains critically limited for many drug discovery tasks. High-quality, experimentally validated datasets for neurodegenerative disease targets, particularly for rare proteins implicated in familial forms of ALS, FTD, or atypical parkinsonian syndromes, are scarce. Data imbalance—where inactive compounds vastly outnumber actives in screening libraries—distorts model optimization and inflates apparent performance metrics. Reproducibility of reported AI benchmark results is frequently compromised by inconsistent data preprocessing protocols, non-standardized splitting strategies, and selective reporting of favorable evaluations. Community-wide efforts toward standardized benchmark suites (e.g., MoleculeNet, ADMET-AI), mandatory deposition of training data and model weights, and rigorous statistical reporting practices are essential to building a reliable scientific foundation.
Model generalization beyond training distributions. A persistent limitation of current AI models in drug discovery is their tendency to perform poorly when extrapolating to molecular or biological regimes substantially different from the training distribution—precisely the scenario encountered during genuine drug discovery, where the most valuable predictions concern novel chemotypes or underexplored target classes. Distribution shift, domain adaptation, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization are active research frontiers, with approaches including meta-learning, conformal prediction for calibrated uncertainty quantification, and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrate thermodynamic and quantum mechanical constraints into model training offering promising avenues.
Blood-brain barrier permeability and CNS drug design. The unique physiological challenge of CNS drug discovery—the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which actively excludes most small molecules from the brain parenchyma through efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP) and tight junction integrity—demands specialized AI models that go beyond conventional lipophilicity-based ADMET prediction. The accurate modeling of P-gp substrate recognition, CNS-specific metabolic stability, and the free fraction available for target engagement in brain tissue remains an unsolved computational problem. Dataset scarcity for CNS pharmacokinetics, arising from the technical difficulty and ethical constraints of in vivo CNS PK studies, further limits model quality.
Causal AI and mechanistic reasoning. Virtually all current AI models in drug discovery are associative rather than causal—they learn statistical correlations in historical data rather than encoding the causal mechanisms governing biological systems. This distinction has profound practical consequences: associative models can fail catastrophically when the causal structure of the domain changes (e.g., in the presence of a disease subtype not represented in training data), and they cannot reliably predict the consequences of interventions that are combinatorially novel. Causal inference frameworks, mechanistic simulations, and hybrid models that integrate data-driven ML with systems biology differential equation models represent a crucial frontier for producing AI predictions that are mechanistically grounded and intervention-aware.
Ethical AI, bias, and equitable drug discovery. AI models trained on historically biased biomedical datasets—where certain patient populations (women, ethnic minorities, elderly individuals) are systematically underrepresented—risk perpetuating and amplifying existing health disparities. The prioritization of economically profitable drug targets over unmet medical needs in neglected populations, and the potential for AI-driven precision medicine approaches to deepen rather than narrow global health inequity, demands proactive engagement with ethical AI frameworks. Diversity-aware dataset construction, fairness-constrained model training, and inclusive stakeholder engagement throughout the AI development lifecycle are necessary conditions for equitable AI-driven drug discovery.
Regulatory pathway for AI-enabled drug discovery. The regulatory framework for AI/ML-based tools in pharmaceutical development is actively evolving, with the FDA's discussion paper on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and the Innovative Science and Technology Approaches for New Drugs (ISTAND) pilot program representing early steps toward a coherent regulatory pathway. The validation, qualification, and transparent documentation of AI models used in drug discovery decision-making will require new standards for model cards, performance benchmarks, and post-deployment monitoring that the regulatory and scientific communities are only beginning to codify.
12. Conclusion
This chapter has presented a comprehensive overview of the AI methodologies that constitute the foundational toolkit for computational drug discovery in the era of biomedical AI. From the rigorous curation of heterogeneous biological data resources, through classical machine learning and deep learning architectures, to graph neural networks, generative models, NLP, multi-omics integration, and privacy-preserving federated approaches, the breadth and depth of AI's impact on drug discovery are now unmistakable.
In the context of neurodegeneration—where the clinical need is acute, the biological complexity is extraordinary, and the historical record of therapeutic failure is sobering—AI offers not a magic solution but a powerful set of analytical and generative tools that, when applied with scientific rigor and domain expertise, can meaningfully accelerate the identification of novel targets, the optimization of lead compounds, and the stratification of patient populations for precision therapeutic approaches. The successes of AlphaFold2 in structural biology, diffusion models in pocket-conditioned molecular generation, federated learning in multi-institutional bioactivity modeling, and LLM-powered knowledge extraction from the biomedical literature collectively represent an inflection point in the computational sciences of drug discovery.
The most impactful AI-driven drug discovery of the coming decade will emerge not from algorithmic innovation alone, but from the thoughtful integration of AI capabilities with deep mechanistic biological insight, rigorous experimental validation, and the lived wisdom of clinicians who understand the complexity of neurodegenerative disease in patients. Researchers who cultivate fluency in both the biological and computational dimensions of this challenge—who can critically evaluate AI model outputs against experimental evidence, design hybrid computational-experimental workflows, and communicate uncertainty and limitations alongside predictions—will be best positioned to translate the promise of AI into clinical benefit for patients suffering from the devastating burden of neurodegenerative disease. It is to this community of future researchers that this chapter is dedicated, as a foundation for the transformative work ahead.

5. Graph Neural Networks: Learning from Molecular Topology

Molecules are inherently graph-structured objects—atoms constitute nodes and covalent bonds constitute edges—making graph neural networks (GNNs) the most theoretically principled representational framewo

rk for molecular machine learning. Unlike SMILES-based or fingerprint-based approaches that encode molecular structure as strings or fixed-length bit vectors, GNNs operate directly on the molecular graph
, allowing the model to learn expressive representations that capture the full topological and chemical context of each atom within its bonding environment.

The foundational GNN operation is the message passing step, formalized by Gilmer et al. (2017) in the Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) framework. In each message passing iteration, every node aggreg

ates feature vectors from its immediate neighbors, transforms the aggregated messages through a learnable function, and updates its own hidden representation. After a specified number of iterations (equi

valent to the maximum interaction distance captured by the network), a graph-level readout function pools node-level representations into a single molecular embedding. This iterative neighborhood aggrega
tion process is closely analogous to the computation of circular fingerprints, but with entirely learnable parameters that adapt to specific prediction tasks.

The MPNN framework has spawned a rich family of architectural variants, each introducing innovations in the message passing, aggregation, or readout steps. The Directed Message Passing Neural Network (D-

MPNN), implemented in the Chemprop library by Yang et al. (2019), passes messages along directed bonds rather than between atoms, preserving stereochemical information and achieving best-in-class perform

ance across 19 molecular property prediction benchmarks spanning physicochemistry, bioactivity, and toxicology. AttentiveFP, developed by Xiong et al. (2020), incorporates graph attention mechanisms that

assign learned importance weights to neighboring atoms, producing interpretable attention maps that highlight pharmacophoric features driving model predictions. Such attention-based interpretability is
particularly valuable in drug discovery contexts where understanding the structural basis of predicted activity is as important as the prediction itself.

The application of GNNs to drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction has yielded particularly impactful results. Models such as GraphDTA represent both drug molecules and protein sequences as graphs (usin

g contact maps derived from protein structures), enabling end-to-end learning of binding affinity from structural information alone. Knowledge graph-based GNNs, exemplified by KGNN, leverage pharmacologi

cal knowledge graphs encoding drug-target-disease-pathway relationships to predict drug-drug interactions (DDIs) with high accuracy. In the domain of neurodegeneration, GNN-based models have been applied
to predict the binding affinity of small molecules to amyloid-beta fibrils, tau protein aggregates, alpha-synuclein oligomers, and therapeutic targets such as BACE1, LRRK2, and parkin.

Beyond single-molecule property prediction, GNNs have been applied to chemical reaction prediction—a critical capability for retrosynthetic planning of drug candidates. The Molecular Transformer and Grap

hRetro models frame reaction prediction as a graph-to-graph transformation problem, enabling AI-assisted synthesis route planning that dramatically accelerates the design-make-test cycle. In protein inte

raction networks, GNNs applied to PPI graphs have identified novel drug target candidates and predicted synergistic drug combinations by modeling the network consequences of multi-target perturbations.

rounds, culminating in global mean pooling and a property prediction output head. The figure should clearly annotate the distinction between atom-level and bond-level features.

6. Natural Language Processing: Mining the Biomedical Literature for Drug Discovery

The biomedical literature constitutes one of the largest and most rapidly expanding repositories of structured scientific knowledge on Earth. PubMed alone indexes over 36 million citations, growing by ap

proximately 1.5 million new publications annually. Embedded within this vast textual resource are data on drug-target interactions, gene-disease associations, adverse drug reactions, protein functions, c

linical trial outcomes, and mechanistic hypotheses—information whose systematic extraction and integration could powerfully accelerate drug discovery. Natural language processing (NLP) provides the compu
tational toolkit for converting unstructured text into machine-interpretable knowledge.

The application of NLP to biomedical text encompasses several distinct tasks. Named entity recognition (NER) involves the automated identification and classification of biomedical entities—genes, protein

s, diseases, drugs, mutations, cell lines—within text. Relation extraction (RE) identifies the semantic relationships between entities, such as 'drug X inhibits protein Y' or 'gene A is upregulated in di

sease B.' Document classification assigns category labels (disease area, experimental modality, study design) to full documents. Question answering (QA) systems respond to natural language queries by ret

rieving and synthesizing relevant passages from the literature corpus. Collectively, these capabilities enable the automated construction of knowledge graphs from the primary literature, supporting targe
t identification, repurposing hypothesis generation, and adverse event signal detection.

The introduction of BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) by Devlin et al. (2018) catalyzed a revolution in NLP by demonstrating that large-scale unsupervised pre-training on vas

t text corpora, followed by task-specific fine-tuning, could achieve state-of-the-art performance across virtually all NLP benchmarks. Domain-specific derivatives—BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), trained on P

ubMed abstracts and PMC full texts; PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021), pre-trained from scratch exclusively on biomedical literature; and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), trained on the broader scientific lite

rature—have substantially improved performance on biomedical NLP tasks relative to general-domain BERT, reflecting the importance of domain-adapted pre-training for capturing specialized scientific vocab

In the context of neurodegeneration, biomedical NLP has been applied to construct disease-specific knowledge graphs integrating gene-disease associations, drug-target interactions, and pathway annotation

s from the PD and AD literature. The SemMedDB knowledge base, derived from semantic predications extracted from all of PubMed by the SemRep NLP system, provides a comprehensive network resource for compu

tational drug repurposing analyses. Natural language-driven drug repurposing studies have identified approved drugs such as sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor), metformin (AMPK activator), and rapamycin (mTOR in
hibitor) as candidate AD therapeutics based on patterns in the literature graph that precede or complement experimental validation.

The chemical dimension of NLP—treating molecular SMILES notation as a chemical language—has given rise to transformer-based molecular property prediction models (MolBERT, ChemBERTa) that achieve competit

ive performance on standard benchmarks without explicit molecular graph construction. More recently, large language models (LLMs) such as BioGPT and Med-PaLM 2 have demonstrated capacity for biomedical r
easoning, pharmacological knowledge retrieval, and structured information extraction that augments expert knowledge in early-stage drug discovery workflows.

Table 5 provides an overview of key NLP tools and models relevant to drug discovery, and Figure 4 (to be created by authors) should illustrate a complete biomedical NLP pipeline for knowledge graph const

ruction from the literature: input documents flow through tokenization, NER, and relation extraction modules, with extracted entity-relation triples assembled into a knowledge graph visualization (nodes

representing drugs, targets, diseases, and pathways; edges labeled with relationship types and confidence scores). The figure should also show a downstream application—for example, a graph query identify
ing drug candidates with known CNS penetrance that modulate a target co-expressed with APP in neurons.

7. Generative AI Models for De Novo Drug Design

The traditional hit discovery paradigm—whether through high-throughput screening of compound libraries, fragment-based drug discovery, or virtual screening of existing chemical databases—is inherently co

nstrained to the exploration of previously synthesized or catalogued chemical space. Generative AI models transcend this limitation by learning the underlying statistical distribution of drug-like molecu

les from training data and using this learned distribution to synthesize entirely new molecular entities with desired pharmacological properties. This paradigm shift from discriminative (predicting prope
rties of given molecules) to generative (designing molecules with target properties) represents one of the most exciting frontiers in computational drug discovery.

Variational autoencoders (VAEs) were among the first deep generative models systematically applied to molecular design. The VAE framework comprises an encoder network that maps input molecules (typically

represented as SMILES strings or molecular graphs) to a continuous, lower-dimensional latent space defined by a multivariate Gaussian distribution, and a decoder network that reconstructs molecules from

sampled latent points. The continuity of the latent space—enforced by the KL-divergence term in the VAE objective—enables smooth interpolation between molecules and gradient-guided optimization toward d

esired property profiles, a process known as Bayesian optimization in latent space. The Junction Tree VAE (JT-VAE) of Jin et al. (2018) addressed the challenge of generating structurally valid molecules
by decomposing molecular graphs into tree-structured scaffolds and side chains, achieving 97.2% molecular validity and enabling scaffold-constrained optimization.

Generative adversarial networks (GANs) approach molecular generation through an adversarial training dynamic: a generator network learns to produce molecular representations that are indistinguishable fr

om real drug-like molecules (as judged by a discriminator network), while the discriminator is simultaneously trained to distinguish real from generated examples. MolGAN, developed by De Cao and Kipf (20

18), combines a GAN framework with graph convolutional networks to directly generate molecular graphs (rather than SMILES strings), bypassing the chemical validity constraints inherent to SMILES generati

on. The druGAN architecture incorporates reinforcement learning signals to bias generation toward compounds with high predicted bioactivity against specific targets, demonstrating the utility of multi-ob
jective optimization in generative drug design.

Diffusion models—a class of generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noise-corruption process—have recently emerged as the most powerful framework for three-dimensional molecular generation cond

itioned on protein binding pockets. DiffSBDD and TargetDiff frame pocket-conditioned molecule generation as a conditional denoising problem, directly generating atomic coordinates and element types of dr

ug candidates anchored to the geometric and electrostatic features of a protein binding site. DiffDock, developed by Corso et al. (2022), reformulates molecular docking itself as a diffusion process over

the space of ligand poses, achieving a 38% improvement in top-1 success rate relative to the previous state-of-the-art blind docking method. These structure-based generative approaches are particularly
relevant for neurodegenerative drug targets with well-characterized binding pockets, including cholinesterases, BACE1, LRRK2, and PERK.

The integration of reinforcement learning (RL) with generative models has enabled multi-objective molecular optimization against simultaneously enforced drug discovery criteria. REINVENT, developed and e

xtensively deployed by AstraZeneca, treats molecular generation as a Markov Decision Process in which an agent (LSTM generator) receives scalar rewards based on a composite scoring function encoding pred

icted bioactivity, ADMET properties, synthetic accessibility, and structural novelty. This approach has proven highly effective in industry-scale lead optimization campaigns and has been applied to CNS-p
enetrant drug design challenges including the simultaneous optimization of BBB permeability, microsomal stability, hERG selectivity, and target potency.

nerated molecules. Use structural formula representations for example inputs and outputs. The figure should also include a property space visualization (QED vs. SA score scatter plot) comparing molecules

8. Multi-Omics Data Integration and AI in Systems-Level Drug Discovery

Neurodegenerative diseases are complex, polygenic, and multifactorial conditions whose molecular pathophysiology cannot be adequately captured at any single biological level. Genomic risk variants identi

fied through GWAS studies affect gene expression (transcriptomics), protein abundance and modification (proteomics), epigenetic regulation (epigenomics), and metabolic flux (metabolomics) in context-spec

ific and cell-type-specific manners. A comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanisms driving neurodegeneration—and the identification of disease-modifying intervention points—therefore demands
the integration of multi-omics data across biological scales and measurement modalities.

Transcriptomic analysis, and in particular single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), has revolutionized our understanding of cellular heterogeneity in the brain. By profiling gene expression in individual

cells rather than bulk tissue, scRNA-seq reveals the distinct transcriptional signatures of neuronal subtypes, glial populations, and immune cells in health and disease. AI methods for scRNA-seq analysis

—including the variational autoencoder-based scVI framework, the diffusion map tool PHATE, and transformer-based cell type classifiers—enable robust dimensionality reduction, trajectory inference, and di

fferential expression analysis at single-cell resolution. In AD and PD research, scRNA-seq studies have identified disease-associated microglia (DAM) subpopulations, vulnerable dopaminergic neuron subtyp
es, and reactive astrocyte states that constitute compelling cellular targets for therapeutic intervention.

Proteomics data generated by mass spectrometry platforms provides direct quantification of protein abundance, post-translational modification (PTM) status, and protein-protein interaction landscapes. The

phosphoproteome—the comprehensive map of phosphorylated serine, threonine, and tyrosine residues—is of particular interest in neurodegeneration, as phosphorylation events on tau, alpha-synuclein, TDP-43

, and FUS are intimately linked to protein aggregation and toxicity. AI models trained on proteomics data have been applied to disease subtype classification, biomarker discovery in cerebrospinal fluid (
CSF) and plasma, and the prediction of proteolytic degradation sequences for targeted protein degradation (PROTAC) design.

The integration of heterogeneous omics layers into unified analytical frameworks presents profound computational challenges. Data matrices from different omics platforms differ fundamentally in scale, di

mensionality, sparsity, and noise characteristics. Classical multi-omics integration approaches—canonical correlation analysis (CCA), multiple co-inertia analysis (MCIA), and MOFA (Multi-Omics Factor Ana

lysis)—provide linear dimensionality reduction and latent factor decomposition across omics layers. Deep learning-based multi-modal autoencoders learn non-linear joint representations by simultaneously e
ncoding multiple omics modalities into a shared latent space, enabling the discovery of cross-modal regulatory relationships that are invisible to single-omics analyses.

Network-based multi-omics integration approaches, which represent genes, proteins, metabolites, and disease phenotypes as nodes in multilayer biological networks, provide a natural framework for systems

pharmacology. The network proximity framework—quantifying the topological closeness between drug targets and disease genes in interactome networks—has been extensively validated as a predictor of drug ef

ficacy and repurposing potential. GNN-based models trained on multilayer omics networks have been applied to predict drug combination synergy, identify essential network hubs as therapeutic targets, and
model the downstream consequences of gene knockouts at the systems level.

9. AI in Target Identification, Validation, and Drug Repurposing for Neurodegeneration

The identification of a valid therapeutic target—a biological molecule whose modulation will produce a disease-relevant pharmacological effect without unacceptable toxicity—is the critical first step of

the drug discovery pipeline and a major source of late-stage attrition. Historically, target identification relied on genetic association studies, phenotypic observations, and hypothesis-driven experimen
tal biology. Artificial intelligence is transforming this process by enabling the systematic, data-driven prioritization of targets across the entire molecular landscape of a disease.

Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of genetic risk loci for AD, PD, and other neurodegenerative diseases, but translating statistical genetic associations into actionable the

rapeutic hypotheses requires sophisticated computational prioritization. Bayesian colocalization analyses, Mendelian randomization, and fine-mapping algorithms disentangle causal variants from linkage di

sequilibrium, while machine learning models trained on transcriptomics data predict the functional consequences of risk variants on gene expression (eQTL effects), splicing (sQTL), and protein abundance

(pQTL). The integration of GWAS summary statistics with tissue-specific expression data through methods such as S-PrediXcan and FUSION enables the prioritization of genetically regulated genes as drug ta
rgets, with those showing evidence of causal involvement rather than mere statistical association receiving the highest priority scores.

Knowledge graph-based target identification leverages the rich semantic connectivity of biomedical knowledge to identify targets at the intersection of disease biology and druggable protein families. Gra

ph machine learning algorithms—including node embedding methods (node2vec, DeepWalk), relational graph convolutional networks (R-GCN), and knowledge graph completion models (TransE, RotatE)—learn vector

representations of biological entities that reflect their functional relationships. Applied to Hetionet or similar disease-specific knowledge graphs, these models predict novel disease-gene associations

with high specificity by identifying gene nodes that are topologically proximate to established disease mechanisms. In the context of neurodegeneration, such approaches have highlighted lysosomal dysfunc
tion, neuroinflammation signaling (particularly TREM2-APOE-TYROBP axis), mitochondrial quality control, and RNA splicing regulation as computationally enriched target spaces.

Drug repurposing—the identification of new therapeutic applications for approved or investigational drugs—offers a compelling risk-reduction strategy given the established safety profiles and pharmacokin

etic characterization of the repurposed candidates. AI-driven repurposing approaches span several methodological classes. Signature-based methods (e.g., using the LINCS L1000 perturbational transcriptomi

cs database) identify drugs whose gene expression signatures are inversely correlated with disease expression profiles, as exemplified by the CMap (Connectivity Map) approach. Network-based repurposing e

xploits the principle that drugs with targets proximal to disease genes in the interactome network are more likely to modulate disease-relevant biology. Deep learning models trained on multi-source drug-
disease interaction data can predict repurposing opportunities with greater accuracy than single-data-type approaches.

Several compelling AI-driven repurposing predictions have been validated experimentally or advanced to clinical trials in the neurodegeneration space. Fang et al. (2022) used a network medicine framework

to identify sildenafil (a PDE5 inhibitor) as a candidate AD drug based on its proximal targeting of tau phosphorylation and amyloid processing networks—a prediction subsequently supported by retrospecti

ve analysis of insurance claims data showing reduced AD incidence in sildenafil users. AI-based analyses of UK Biobank data have identified metformin, losartan, and several repurposed anti-inflammatory a
gents as associated with reduced AD risk, generating testable repurposing hypotheses that are now being pursued in clinical trials.

lti-omics data inputs (GWAS, transcriptomics, proteomics), flowing through a knowledge graph integration layer, processed by a GNN-based target prioritization model, and outputting a ranked list of candi

date targets annotated by druggability score, genetic evidence tier, and tissue expression profile. Include a sidebar showing the network proximity concept—a bipartite graph with drug targets on one side

10. Explainable AI and Federated Learning: Toward Trustworthy Drug Discovery

The increasing deployment of deep learning models in high-stakes drug discovery decisions has intensified scrutiny of model interpretability and trustworthiness. Regulatory agencies, including the US Foo

d and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Medicines Agency (EMA), have issued guidance emphasizing the importance of model transparency, bias assessment, and audit trails for AI systems used in ph

armaceutical development. The field of explainable AI (XAI) addresses these imperatives by developing methods that illuminate the reasoning processes of complex models, enabling scientists to verify that
predictions are grounded in chemically meaningful features rather than spurious correlations.

Model-agnostic XAI methods—applicable regardless of the underlying model architecture—include LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), which approximates a complex model locally with a sim

pler interpretable surrogate, and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), which attributes individual model output values to each input feature based on game-theoretic Shapley values. In molecular property

prediction, SHAP has been applied to identify the specific atoms, bonds, and substructural fragments most responsible for predicted ADMET properties, enabling medicinal chemists to design targeted struc

tural modifications that improve a desired property while minimizing collateral impacts. AttentiveFP's graph attention mechanism provides intrinsic interpretability by directly assigning learned importan
ce weights to atoms and bonds, producing visualizable attribution maps that highlight pharmacophoric features driving activity predictions.

For protein structure-based models, gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) and integrated gradients methods can highlight the residues in a binding pocket that most strongly influence pred

icted binding affinity or selectivity, directly guiding structure-based optimization. In biomedical NLP, attention visualization tools applied to BioBERT and PubMedBERT models reveal which token pairs in

a text the model uses to support relation extraction predictions, enabling human experts to validate or correct the model's semantic reasoning. The development of XAI-enhanced dashboards for drug discov

ery workflows—integrating predictions, uncertainty estimates, and attribution maps in a single decision-support interface—is an active area of tool development by both academic groups and pharmaceutical

Federated learning (FL) addresses a complementary challenge in AI-driven drug discovery: the need to train models on large, diverse datasets while respecting data privacy, proprietary ownership, and regu

latory constraints that preclude the centralization of sensitive patient data or confidential assay results. In the federated learning paradigm, a global model is trained collaboratively across multiple

institutions or organizations, with each participant computing gradients on their local data and contributing only aggregated parameter updates—never raw data—to the central model server. This architectu
re enables the pooling of statistical power across institutions without the privacy, security, and IP risks of data centralization.

The MELLODDY consortium (Machine Learning Ledger Orchestration for Drug Discovery), a landmark industry-academia collaboration spanning ten major pharmaceutical companies, demonstrated the feasibility an

d substantial performance gains of privacy-preserving federated multi-task learning for bioactivity prediction. By training a shared deep neural network across the pooled compound-activity data of all co

nsortium members—without any company disclosing proprietary structures or assay data to competitors—the MELLODDY model substantially outperformed models trained on individual company datasets alone, part

icularly for orphan targets with limited in-house data coverage. This proof-of-concept has catalyzed ongoing federated learning initiatives in neurodegenerative disease research, where multi-center patie
nt cohorts are essential for capturing the full biological heterogeneity of these conditions.

Figure 8 (to be created by authors) should provide a side-by-side visual comparison of centralized versus federated learning architectures for drug discovery: the left panel should show the traditional c

entralized approach (data from multiple hospitals/pharma companies flowing to a single central server, training a unified model), while the right panel depicts the federated approach (each institution tr

ains locally, only model parameters flow to the central aggregator, data remains siloed). Annotate privacy-preserving mechanisms (differential privacy noise injection, secure aggregation) and show the gl
obal model update cycle. Use institution icons specific to pharmaceutical and clinical settings.

Despite the transformative advances documented in this chapter, several fundamental challenges constrain the full realization of AI's potential in drug discovery, particularly for neurodegenerative disea

ses. Addressing these challenges will require concerted effort from the computational, experimental, and clinical research communities, as well as thoughtful engagement with regulatory bodies, ethics exp

Data scarcity, imbalance, and reproducibility. The performance of supervised machine learning models scales with the quantity and quality of labeled training data—a resource that remains critically limit

ed for many drug discovery tasks. High-quality, experimentally validated datasets for neurodegenerative disease targets, particularly for rare proteins implicated in familial forms of ALS, FTD, or atypic

al parkinsonian syndromes, are scarce. Data imbalance—where inactive compounds vastly outnumber actives in screening libraries—distorts model optimization and inflates apparent performance metrics. Repro

ducibility of reported AI benchmark results is frequently compromised by inconsistent data preprocessing protocols, non-standardized splitting strategies, and selective reporting of favorable evaluations

. Community-wide efforts toward standardized benchmark suites (e.g., MoleculeNet, ADMET-AI), mandatory deposition of training data and model weights, and rigorous statistical reporting practices are esse
ntial to building a reliable scientific foundation.

Model generalization beyond training distributions. A persistent limitation of current AI models in drug discovery is their tendency to perform poorly when extrapolating to molecular or biological regime

s substantially different from the training distribution—precisely the scenario encountered during genuine drug discovery, where the most valuable predictions concern novel chemotypes or underexplored ta

rget classes. Distribution shift, domain adaptation, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization are active research frontiers, with approaches including meta-learning, conformal prediction for calibrat
ed uncertainty quantification, and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrate thermodynamic and quantum mechanical constraints into model training offering promising avenues.

Blood-brain barrier permeability and CNS drug design. The unique physiological challenge of CNS drug discovery—the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which actively excludes most small molecules from the brain p

arenchyma through efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP) and tight junction integrity—demands specialized AI models that go beyond conventional lipophilicity-based ADMET prediction. The accurate modeling of P-

gp substrate recognition, CNS-specific metabolic stability, and the free fraction available for target engagement in brain tissue remains an unsolved computational problem. Dataset scarcity for CNS pharm
acokinetics, arising from the technical difficulty and ethical constraints of in vivo CNS PK studies, further limits model quality.

Causal AI and mechanistic reasoning. Virtually all current AI models in drug discovery are associative rather than causal—they learn statistical correlations in historical data rather than encoding the c

ausal mechanisms governing biological systems. This distinction has profound practical consequences: associative models can fail catastrophically when the causal structure of the domain changes (e.g., in

the presence of a disease subtype not represented in training data), and they cannot reliably predict the consequences of interventions that are combinatorially novel. Causal inference frameworks, mecha

nistic simulations, and hybrid models that integrate data-driven ML with systems biology differential equation models represent a crucial frontier for producing AI predictions that are mechanistically gr

Ethical AI, bias, and equitable drug discovery. AI models trained on historically biased biomedical datasets—where certain patient populations (women, ethnic minorities, elderly individuals) are systemat

ically underrepresented—risk perpetuating and amplifying existing health disparities. The prioritization of economically profitable drug targets over unmet medical needs in neglected populations, and the

potential for AI-driven precision medicine approaches to deepen rather than narrow global health inequity, demands proactive engagement with ethical AI frameworks. Diversity-aware dataset construction,
fairness-constrained model training, and inclusive stakeholder engagement throughout the AI development lifecycle are necessary conditions for equitable AI-driven drug discovery.

Regulatory pathway for AI-enabled drug discovery. The regulatory framework for AI/ML-based tools in pharmaceutical development is actively evolving, with the FDA's discussion paper on AI/ML-based Softwar

e as a Medical Device (SaMD) and the Innovative Science and Technology Approaches for New Drugs (ISTAND) pilot program representing early steps toward a coherent regulatory pathway. The validation, quali

fication, and transparent documentation of AI models used in drug discovery decision-making will require new standards for model cards, performance benchmarks, and post-deployment monitoring that the reg
ulatory and scientific communities are only beginning to codify.

This chapter has presented a comprehensive overview of the AI methodologies that constitute the foundational toolkit for computational drug discovery in the era of biomedical AI. From the rigorous curati

on of heterogeneous biological data resources, through classical machine learning and deep learning architectures, to graph neural networks, generative models, NLP, multi-omics integration, and privacy-p
reserving federated approaches, the breadth and depth of AI's impact on drug discovery are now unmistakable.

In the context of neurodegeneration—where the clinical need is acute, the biological complexity is extraordinary, and the historical record of therapeutic failure is sobering—AI offers not a magic soluti

on but a powerful set of analytical and generative tools that, when applied with scientific rigor and domain expertise, can meaningfully accelerate the identification of novel targets, the optimization o

f lead compounds, and the stratification of patient populations for precision therapeutic approaches. The successes of AlphaFold2 in structural biology, diffusion models in pocket-conditioned molecular g

eneration, federated learning in multi-institutional bioactivity modeling, and LLM-powered knowledge extraction from the biomedical literature collectively represent an inflection point in the computatio

The most impactful AI-driven drug discovery of the coming decade will emerge not from algorithmic innovation alone, but from the thoughtful integration of AI capabilities with deep mechanistic biological

insight, rigorous experimental validation, and the lived wisdom of clinicians who understand the complexity of neurodegenerative disease in patients. Researchers who cultivate fluency in both the biolog

ical and computational dimensions of this challenge—who can critically evaluate AI model outputs against experimental evidence, design hybrid computational-experimental workflows, and communicate uncerta

inty and limitations alongside predictions—will be best positioned to translate the promise of AI into clinical benefit for patients suffering from the devastating burden of neurodegenerative disease. It
is to this community of future researchers that this chapter is dedicated, as a foundation for the transformative work ahead.

5. Graph Neural Networks: Learning from Molecular Topology
Molecules are inherently graph-structured objects—atoms constitute nodes and covalent bonds constitute edges—making graph neural networks (GNNs) the most theoretically principled representational framework for molecular machine learning. Unlike SMILES-based or fingerprint-based approaches that encode molecular structure as strings or fixed-length bit vectors, GNNs operate directly on the molecular graph, allowing the model to learn expressive representations that capture the full topological and chemical context of each atom within its bonding environment.
The foundational GNN operation is the message passing step, formalized by Gilmer et al. (2017) in the Message Passing Neural Network (MPNN) framework. In each message passing iteration, every node aggregates feature vectors from its immediate neighbors, transforms the aggregated messages through a learnable function, and updates its own hidden representation. After a specified number of iterations (equivalent to the maximum interaction distance captured by the network), a graph-level readout function pools node-level representations into a single molecular embedding. This iterative neighborhood aggregation process is closely analogous to the computation of circular fingerprints, but with entirely learnable parameters that adapt to specific prediction tasks.
The MPNN framework has spawned a rich family of architectural variants, each introducing innovations in the message passing, aggregation, or readout steps. The Directed Message Passing Neural Network (D-MPNN), implemented in the Chemprop library by Yang et al. (2019), passes messages along directed bonds rather than between atoms, preserving stereochemical information and achieving best-in-class performance across 19 molecular property prediction benchmarks spanning physicochemistry, bioactivity, and toxicology. AttentiveFP, developed by Xiong et al. (2020), incorporates graph attention mechanisms that assign learned importance weights to neighboring atoms, producing interpretable attention maps that highlight pharmacophoric features driving model predictions. Such attention-based interpretability is particularly valuable in drug discovery contexts where understanding the structural basis of predicted activity is as important as the prediction itself.
The application of GNNs to drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction has yielded particularly impactful results. Models such as GraphDTA represent both drug molecules and protein sequences as graphs (using contact maps derived from protein structures), enabling end-to-end learning of binding affinity from structural information alone. Knowledge graph-based GNNs, exemplified by KGNN, leverage pharmacological knowledge graphs encoding drug-target-disease-pathway relationships to predict drug-drug interactions (DDIs) with high accuracy. In the domain of neurodegeneration, GNN-based models have been applied to predict the binding affinity of small molecules to amyloid-beta fibrils, tau protein aggregates, alpha-synuclein oligomers, and therapeutic targets such as BACE1, LRRK2, and parkin.
Beyond single-molecule property prediction, GNNs have been applied to chemical reaction prediction—a critical capability for retrosynthetic planning of drug candidates. The Molecular Transformer and GraphRetro models frame reaction prediction as a graph-to-graph transformation problem, enabling AI-assisted synthesis route planning that dramatically accelerates the design-make-test cycle. In protein interaction networks, GNNs applied to PPI graphs have identified novel drug target candidates and predicted synergistic drug combinations by modeling the network consequences of multi-target perturbations.  
Table 4 summarizes representative GNN models for drug-target interaction prediction, and Figure 3 (to be created by authors) should depict a schematic of the message passing mechanism in a GNN applied to a drug molecule: atoms shown as labeled nodes (element type, formal charge, hybridization), bonds as edges (bond type encoded), with arrows illustrating the iterative message passing process across two rounds, culminating in global mean pooling and a property prediction output head. 6. Natural Language Processing: Mining the Biomedical Literature for Drug Discovery
The biomedical literature constitutes one of the largest and most rapidly expanding repositories of structured scientific knowledge on Earth. PubMed alone indexes over 36 million citations, growing by approximately 1.5 million new publications annually. Embedded within this vast textual resource are data on drug-target interactions, gene-disease associations, adverse drug reactions, protein functions, clinical trial outcomes, and mechanistic hypotheses—information whose systematic extraction and integration could powerfully accelerate drug discovery. Natural language processing (NLP) provides the computational toolkit for converting unstructured text into machine-interpretable knowledge.
The application of NLP to biomedical text encompasses several distinct tasks. Named entity recognition (NER) involves the automated identification and classification of biomedical entities—genes, proteins, diseases, drugs, mutations, cell lines—within text. Relation extraction (RE) identifies the semantic relationships between entities, such as 'drug X inhibits protein Y' or 'gene A is upregulated in disease B.' Document classification assigns category labels (disease area, experimental modality, study design) to full documents. Question answering (QA) systems respond to natural language queries by retrieving and synthesizing relevant passages from the literature corpus. Collectively, these capabilities enable the automated construction of knowledge graphs from the primary literature, supporting target identification, repurposing hypothesis generation, and adverse event signal detection.
The introduction of BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) by Devlin et al. (2018) catalyzed a revolution in NLP by demonstrating that large-scale unsupervised pre-training on vast text corpora, followed by task-specific fine-tuning, could achieve state-of-the-art performance across virtually all NLP benchmarks. Domain-specific derivatives—BioBERT (Lee et al., 2020), trained on PubMed abstracts and PMC full texts; PubMedBERT (Gu et al., 2021), pre-trained from scratch exclusively on biomedical literature; and SciBERT (Beltagy et al., 2019), trained on the broader scientific literature—have substantially improved performance on biomedical NLP tasks relative to general-domain BERT, reflecting the importance of domain-adapted pre-training for capturing specialized scientific vocabulary and discourse patterns.
In the context of neurodegeneration, biomedical NLP has been applied to construct disease-specific knowledge graphs integrating gene-disease associations, drug-target interactions, and pathway annotations from the PD and AD literature. The SemMedDB knowledge base, derived from semantic predications ex

... [3,639 characters omitted] ...

AE) of Jin et al. (2018) addressed the challenge of generating structurally valid molecules by decomposing molecular graphs into tree-structured scaffolds and side chains, achieving 97.2% molecular validity and enabling scaffold-constrained optimization.
Generative adversarial networks (GANs) approach molecular generation through an adversarial training dynamic: a generator network learns to produce molecular representations that are indistinguishable from real drug-like molecules (as judged by a discriminator network), while the discriminator is simultaneously trained to distinguish real from generated examples. MolGAN, developed by De Cao and Kipf (2018), combines a GAN framework with graph convolutional networks to directly generate molecular graphs (rather than SMILES strings), bypassing the chemical validity constraints inherent to SMILES generation. The druGAN architecture incorporates reinforcement learning signals to bias generation toward compounds with high predicted bioactivity against specific targets, demonstrating the utility of multi-objective optimization in generative drug design.
Diffusion models—a class of generative models that learn to reverse a gradual noise-corruption process—have recently emerged as the most powerful framework for three-dimensional molecular generation conditioned on protein binding pockets. DiffSBDD and TargetDiff frame pocket-conditioned molecule generation as a conditional denoising problem, directly generating atomic coordinates and element types of drug candidates anchored to the geometric and electrostatic features of a protein binding site. DiffDock, developed by Corso et al. (2022), reformulates molecular docking itself as a diffusion process over the space of ligand poses, achieving a 38% improvement in top-1 success rate relative to the previous state-of-the-art blind docking method. These structure-based generative approaches are particularly relevant for neurodegenerative drug targets with well-characterized binding pockets, including cholinesterases, BACE1, LRRK2, and PERK.
The integration of reinforcement learning (RL) with generative models has enabled multi-objective molecular optimization against simultaneously enforced drug discovery criteria. REINVENT, developed and extensively deployed by AstraZeneca, treats molecular generation as a Markov Decision Process in which an agent (LSTM generator) receives scalar rewards based on a composite scoring function encoding predicted bioactivity, ADMET properties, synthetic accessibility, and structural novelty. This approach has proven highly effective in industry-scale lead optimization campaigns and has been applied to CNS-penetrant drug design challenges including the simultaneous optimization of BBB permeability, microsomal stability, hERG selectivity, and target potency.

SELFIES = Self-Referencing Embedded Strings, a more robust molecular notation. Performance metrics are task- and dataset-dependent.

8. Multi-Omics Data Integration and AI in Systems-Level Drug Discovery
Neurodegenerative diseases are complex, polygenic, and multifactorial conditions whose molecular pathophysiology cannot be adequately captured at any single biological level. Genomic risk variants identified through GWAS studies affect gene expression (transcriptomics), protein abundance and modification (proteomics), epigenetic regulation (epigenomics), and metabolic flux (metabolomics) in context-specific and cell-type-specific manners. A comprehensive understanding of the molecular mechanisms driving neurodegeneration—and the identification of disease-modifying intervention points—therefore demands the integration of multi-omics data across biological scales and measurement modalities.
Transcriptomic analysis, and in particular single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), has revolutionized our understanding of cellular heterogeneity in the brain. By profiling gene expression in individual cells rather than bulk tissue, scRNA-seq reveals the distinct transcriptional signatures of neuronal subtypes, glial populations, and immune cells in health and disease. AI methods for scRNA-seq analysis—including the variational autoencoder-based scVI framework, the diffusion map tool PHATE, and transformer-based cell type classifiers—enable robust dimensionality reduction, trajectory inference, and differential expression analysis at single-cell resolution. In AD and PD research, scRNA-seq studies have identified disease-associated microglia (DAM) subpopulations, vulnerable dopaminergic neuron subtypes, and reactive astrocyte states that constitute compelling cellular targets for therapeutic intervention.
Proteomics data generated by mass spectrometry platforms provides direct quantification of protein abundance, post-translational modification (PTM) status, and protein-protein interaction landscapes. The phosphoproteome—the comprehensive map of phosphorylated serine, threonine, and tyrosine residues—is of particular interest in neurodegeneration, as phosphorylation events on tau, alpha-synuclein, TDP-43, and FUS are intimately linked to protein aggregation and toxicity. AI models trained on proteomics data have been applied to disease subtype classification, biomarker discovery in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and plasma, and the prediction of proteolytic degradation sequences for targeted protein degradation (PROTAC) design.
The integration of heterogeneous omics layers into unified analytical frameworks presents profound computational challenges. Data matrices from different omics platforms differ fundamentally in scale, dimensionality, sparsity, and noise characteristics. Classical multi-omics integration approaches—canonical correlation analysis (CCA), multiple co-inertia analysis (MCIA), and MOFA (Multi-Omics Factor Analysis)—provide linear dimensionality reduction and latent factor decomposition across omics layers. Deep learning-based multi-modal autoencoders learn non-linear joint representations by simultaneously encoding multiple omics modalities into a shared latent space, enabling the discovery of cross-modal regulatory relationships that are invisible to single-omics analyses.
Network-based multi-omics integration approaches, which represent genes, proteins, metabolites, and disease phenotypes as nodes in multilayer biological networks, provide a natural framework for systems pharmacology. The network proximity framework—quantifying the topological closeness between drug targets and disease genes in interactome networks—has been extensively validated as a predictor of drug efficacy and repurposing potential. GNN-based models trained on multilayer omics networks have been applied to predict drug combination synergy, identify essential network hubs as therapeutic targets, and model the downstream consequences of gene knockouts at the systems level.

9. AI in Target Identification, Validation, and Drug Repurposing for Neurodegeneration
The identification of a valid therapeutic target—a biological molecule whose modulation will produce a disease-relevant pharmacological effect without unacceptable toxicity—is the critical first step of the drug discovery pipeline and a major source of late-stage attrition. Historically, target identification relied on genetic association studies, phenotypic observations, and hypothesis-driven experimental biology. Artificial intelligence is transforming this process by enabling the systematic, data-driven prioritization of targets across the entire molecular landscape of a disease.
Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified hundreds of genetic risk loci for AD, PD, and other neurodegenerative diseases, but translating statistical genetic associations into actionable therapeutic hypotheses requires sophisticated computational prioritization. Bayesian colocalization analyses, Mendelian randomization, and fine-mapping algorithms disentangle causal variants from linkage disequilibrium, while machine learning models trained on transcriptomics data predict the functional consequences of risk variants on gene expression (eQTL effects), splicing (sQTL), and protein abundance (pQTL). The integration of GWAS summary statistics with tissue-specific expression data through methods such as S-PrediXcan and FUSION enables the prioritization of genetically regulated genes as drug targets, with those showing evidence of causal involvement rather than mere statistical association receiving the highest priority scores.
Knowledge graph-based target identification leverages the rich semantic connectivity of biomedical knowledge to identify targets at the intersection of disease biology and druggable protein families. Graph machine learning algorithms—including node embedding methods (node2vec, DeepWalk), relational graph convolutional networks (R-GCN), and knowledge graph completion models (TransE, RotatE)—learn vector representations of biological entities that reflect their functional relationships. Applied to Hetionet or similar disease-specific knowledge graphs, these models predict novel disease-gene associations with high specificity by identifying gene nodes that are topologically proximate to established disease mechanisms. In the context of neurodegeneration, such approaches have highlighted lysosomal dysfunction, neuroinflammation signaling (particularly TREM2-APOE-TYROBP axis), mitochondrial quality control, and RNA splicing regulation as computationally enriched target spaces.
Drug repurposing—the identification of new therapeutic applications for approved or investigational drugs—offers a compelling risk-reduction strategy given the established safety profiles and pharmacokinetic characterization of the repurposed candidates. AI-driven repurposing approaches span several methodological classes. Signature-based methods (e.g., using the LINCS L1000 perturbational transcriptomics database) identify drugs whose gene expression signatures are inversely correlated with disease expression profiles, as exemplified by the CMap (Connectivity Map) approach. Network-based repurposing exploits the principle that drugs with targets proximal to disease genes in the interactome network are more likely to modulate disease-relevant biology. Deep learning models trained on multi-source drug-disease interaction data can predict repurposing opportunities with greater accuracy than single-data-type approaches.
Several compelling AI-driven repurposing predictions have been validated experimentally or advanced to clinical trials in the neurodegeneration space. Fang et al. (2022) used a network medicine framework to identify sildenafil (a PDE5 inhibitor) as a candidate AD drug based on its proximal targeting of tau phosphorylation and amyloid processing networks—a prediction subsequently supported by retrospective analysis of insurance claims data showing reduced AD incidence in sildenafil users. AI-based analyses of UK Biobank data have identified metformin, losartan, and several repurposed anti-inflammatory agents as associated with reduced AD risk, generating testable repurposing hypotheses that are now being pursued in clinical trials.
