10. Explainable AI and Federated Learning: Toward Trustworthy Drug Discovery
The increasing deployment of deep learning models in high-stakes drug discovery decisions has intensified scrutiny of model interpretability and trustworthiness. Regulatory agencies, including the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Medicines Agency (EMA), have issued guidance emphasizing the importance of model transparency, bias assessment, and audit trails for AI systems used in pharmaceutical development. The field of explainable AI (XAI) addresses these imperatives by developing methods that illuminate the reasoning processes of complex models, enabling scientists to verify that predictions are grounded in chemically meaningful features rather than spurious correlations.
Model-agnostic XAI methods—applicable regardless of the underlying model architecture—include LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), which approximates a complex model locally with a simpler interpretable surrogate, and SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), which attributes individual model output values to each input feature based on game-theoretic Shapley values. In molecular property prediction, SHAP has been applied to identify the specific atoms, bonds, and substructural fragments most responsible for predicted ADMET properties, enabling medicinal chemists to design targeted structural modifications that improve a desired property while minimizing collateral impacts. AttentiveFP's graph attention mechanism provides intrinsic interpretability by directly assigning learned importance weights to atoms and bonds, producing visualizable attribution maps that highlight pharmacophoric features driving activity predictions.
For protein structure-based models, gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) and integrated gradients methods can highlight the residues in a binding pocket that most strongly influence predicted binding affinity or selectivity, directly guiding structure-based optimization. In biomedical NLP, attention visualization tools applied to BioBERT and PubMedBERT models reveal which token pairs in a text the model uses to support relation extraction predictions, enabling human experts to validate or correct the model's semantic reasoning. The development of XAI-enhanced dashboards for drug discovery workflows—integrating predictions, uncertainty estimates, and attribution maps in a single decision-support interface—is an active area of tool development by both academic groups and pharmaceutical informatics teams.
Federated learning (FL) addresses a complementary challenge in AI-driven drug discovery: the need to train models on large, diverse datasets while respecting data privacy, proprietary ownership, and regulatory constraints that preclude the centralization of sensitive patient data or confidential assay results. In the federated learning paradigm, a global model is trained collaboratively across multiple institutions or organizations, with each participant computing gradients on their local data and contributing only aggregated parameter updates—never raw data—to the central model server. This architecture enables the pooling of statistical power across institutions without the privacy, security, and IP risks of data centralization.
The MELLODDY consortium (Machine Learning Ledger Orchestration for Drug Discovery), a landmark industry-academia collaboration spanning ten major pharmaceutical companies, demonstrated the feasibility and substantial performance gains of privacy-preserving federated multi-task learning for bioactivity prediction. By training a shared deep neural network across the pooled compound-activity data of all consortium members—without any company disclosing proprietary structures or assay data to competitors—the MELLODDY model substantially outperformed models trained on individual company datasets alone, particularly for orphan targets with limited in-house data coverage. This proof-of-concept has catalyzed ongoing federated learning initiatives in neurodegenerative disease research, where multi-center patient cohorts are essential for capturing the full biological heterogeneity of these conditions.
Figure 8 (to be created by authors) should provide a side-by-side visual comparison of centralized versus federated learning architectures for drug discovery: the left panel should show the traditional centralized approach (data from multiple hospitals/pharma companies flowing to a single central server, training a unified model), while the right panel depicts the federated approach (each institution trains locally, only model parameters flow to the central aggregator, data remains siloed). Annotate privacy-preserving mechanisms (differential privacy noise injection, secure aggregation) and show the global model update cycle. Use institution icons specific to pharmaceutical and clinical settings.
11. Future Challenges and Research Frontiers
Despite the transformative advances documented in this chapter, several fundamental challenges constrain the full realization of AI's potential in drug discovery, particularly for neurodegenerative diseases. Addressing these challenges will require concerted effort from the computational, experimental, and clinical research communities, as well as thoughtful engagement with regulatory bodies, ethics experts, and patient advocacy organizations.
Data scarcity, imbalance, and reproducibility. The performance of supervised machine learning models scales with the quantity and quality of labeled training data—a resource that remains critically limited for many drug discovery tasks. High-quality, experimentally validated datasets for neurodegenerative disease targets, particularly for rare proteins implicated in familial forms of ALS, FTD, or atypical parkinsonian syndromes, are scarce. Data imbalance—where inactive compounds vastly outnumber actives in screening libraries—distorts model optimization and inflates apparent performance metrics. Reproducibility of reported AI benchmark results is frequently compromised by inconsistent data preprocessing protocols, non-standardized splitting strategies, and selective reporting of favorable evaluations. Community-wide efforts toward standardized benchmark suites (e.g., MoleculeNet, ADMET-AI), mandatory deposition of training data and model weights, and rigorous statistical reporting practices are essential to building a reliable scientific foundation.
Model generalization beyond training distributions. A persistent limitation of current AI models in drug discovery is their tendency to perform poorly when extrapolating to molecular or biological regimes substantially different from the training distribution—precisely the scenario encountered during genuine drug discovery, where the most valuable predictions concern novel chemotypes or underexplored target classes. Distribution shift, domain adaptation, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization are active research frontiers, with approaches including meta-learning, conformal prediction for calibrated uncertainty quantification, and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that integrate thermodynamic and quantum mechanical constraints into model training offering promising avenues.
Blood-brain barrier permeability and CNS drug design. The unique physiological challenge of CNS drug discovery—the blood-brain barrier (BBB), which actively excludes most small molecules from the brain parenchyma through efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP) and tight junction integrity—demands specialized AI models that go beyond conventional lipophilicity-based ADMET prediction. The accurate modeling of P-gp substrate recognition, CNS-specific metabolic stability, and the free fraction available for target engagement in brain tissue remains an unsolved computational problem. Dataset scarcity for CNS pharmacokinetics, arising from the technical difficulty and ethical constraints of in vivo CNS PK studies, further limits model quality.
Causal AI and mechanistic reasoning. Virtually all current AI models in drug discovery are associative rather than causal—they learn statistical correlations in historical data rather than encoding the causal mechanisms governing biological systems. This distinction has profound practical consequences: associative models can fail catastrophically when the causal structure of the domain changes (e.g., in the presence of a disease subtype not represented in training data), and they cannot reliably predict the consequences of interventions that are combinatorially novel. Causal inference frameworks, mechanistic simulations, and hybrid models that integrate data-driven ML with systems biology differential equation models represent a crucial frontier for producing AI predictions that are mechanistically grounded and intervention-aware.
Ethical AI, bias, and equitable drug discovery. AI models trained on historically biased biomedical datasets—where certain patient populations (women, ethnic minorities, elderly individuals) are systematically underrepresented—risk perpetuating and amplifying existing health disparities. The prioritization of economically profitable drug targets over unmet medical needs in neglected populations, and the potential for AI-driven precision medicine approaches to deepen rather than narrow global health inequity, demands proactive engagement with ethical AI frameworks. Diversity-aware dataset construction, fairness-constrained model training, and inclusive stakeholder engagement throughout the AI development lifecycle are necessary conditions for equitable AI-driven drug discovery.
Regulatory pathway for AI-enabled drug discovery. The regulatory framework for AI/ML-based tools in pharmaceutical development is actively evolving, with the FDA's discussion paper on AI/ML-based Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and the Innovative Science and Technology Approaches for New Drugs (ISTAND) pilot program representing early steps toward a coherent regulatory pathway. The validation, qualification, and transparent documentation of AI models used in drug discovery decision-making will require new standards for model cards, performance benchmarks, and post-deployment monitoring that the regulatory and scientific communities are only beginning to codify.
12. Conclusion
This chapter has presented a comprehensive overview of the AI methodologies that constitute the foundational toolkit for computational drug discovery in the era of biomedical AI. From the rigorous curation of heterogeneous biological data resources, through classical machine learning and deep learning architectures, to graph neural networks, generative models, NLP, multi-omics integration, and privacy-preserving federated approaches, the breadth and depth of AI's impact on drug discovery are now unmistakable.
In the context of neurodegeneration—where the clinical need is acute, the biological complexity is extraordinary, and the historical record of therapeutic failure is sobering—AI offers not a magic solution but a powerful set of analytical and generative tools that, when applied with scientific rigor and domain expertise, can meaningfully accelerate the identification of novel targets, the optimization of lead compounds, and the stratification of patient populations for precision therapeutic approaches. The successes of AlphaFold2 in structural biology, diffusion models in pocket-conditioned molecular generation, federated learning in multi-institutional bioactivity modeling, and LLM-powered knowledge extraction from the biomedical literature collectively represent an inflection point in the computational sciences of drug discovery.
The most impactful AI-driven drug discovery of the coming decade will emerge not from algorithmic innovation alone, but from the thoughtful integration of AI capabilities with deep mechanistic biological insight, rigorous experimental validation, and the lived wisdom of clinicians who understand the complexity of neurodegenerative disease in patients. Researchers who cultivate fluency in both the biological and computational dimensions of this challenge—who can critically evaluate AI model outputs against experimental evidence, design hybrid computational-experimental workflows, and communicate uncertainty and limitations alongside predictions—will be best positioned to translate the promise of AI into clinical benefit for patients suffering from the devastating burden of neurodegenerative disease. It is to this community of future researchers that this chapter is dedicated, as a foundation for the transformative work ahead.

Figure Legends and Construction Guidance
Figure 1. The AI Drug Discovery Data Ecosystem
Description: A multi-level schematic connecting primary data sources to AI model inputs and drug discovery outputs. Construction: Use BioRender or Adobe Illustrator. Data sources (genomics, proteomics, cheminformatics, clinical) in colored boxes at the top. Preprocessing steps (normalization, featurization, splitting) as mid-layer chevrons. Bottom row: model outputs (target ID, ADMET prediction, virtual screening, molecular generation). Recommended color coding: genomics = blue, proteomics = green, cheminformatics = orange, clinical = purple, AI models = dark teal. Size: Full-page width (~17 cm). Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 2. Deep Learning ADMET Prediction Workflow
Description: Step-by-step pipeline from SMILES input to multi-task ADMET output with uncertainty bars. Construction: Flowchart in draw.io or Affinity Designer. Panels: (A) SMILES string input → molecular graph construction → GNN encoder; (B) Multi-task output heads for solubility, BBB permeability, metabolic stability, hERG inhibition; (C) Calibrated prediction intervals. Label all layers (embedding dimension, activation functions). Recommended color: gradient from light blue (input) to red-orange (output heads). Size: ~14 cm wide × 10 cm tall. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 3. Message Passing in a Molecular Graph Neural Network
Description: Two-round MPNN illustration on a representative drug molecule (e.g., donepezil). Construction: BioRender or custom Python (matplotlib + RDKit for structure rendering). Nodes colored by atom type (C = grey, N = blue, O = red, S = yellow); edges by bond type (single = thin, double = thick, aromatic = dashed). Arrows show message aggregation direction in Round 1 and Round 2. Pooling step shown as node collapse to single vector. Final prediction output box labeled 'Predicted binding affinity: X nM.' Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 4. Biomedical NLP Pipeline for Knowledge Graph Construction
Description: End-to-end pipeline from PubMed text to disease knowledge graph. Construction: draw.io. Left side: raw text input (journal abstract) → tokenizer → NER (highlighted entities) → RE (relation triples). Center: triple extraction examples (drug–inhibits–target; target–associated with–disease). Right: knowledge graph visualization with node types in different colors (drugs = hexagon, proteins = circle, diseases = diamond, pathways = rectangle). Show downstream drug repurposing query as inset. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 5. Comparison of Generative AI Frameworks for Molecular Design
Description: Three-panel figure comparing VAE, GAN, and diffusion model frameworks. Panel A (VAE): Encoder → latent Gaussian distribution → decoder → optimized SMILES. Panel B (GAN): Generator + Discriminator adversarial loop; sampled molecules shown as structural formulas. Panel C (Diffusion model): Forward noise addition on 3D molecular coordinates → reverse denoising conditioned on protein pocket surface. Each panel includes a QED/SA score distribution plot for generated molecules vs. training set. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 6. Multi-Omics Integration Framework for AD Target Discovery
Description: Vertically stacked omics layers (genome → epigenome → transcriptome → proteome → metabolome) with inter-layer arrows. Below: multi-modal autoencoder compressing all layers into shared latent space (Z). Downstream outputs: patient stratification heatmap, biomarker volcano plot, target prioritization ranked list. Callout boxes highlighting AD-specific findings (e.g., APOE4 effect on BIN1 splicing, TREM2 expression in DAM cells). Recommended software: BioRender or Inkscape. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 7. AI-Driven Target Identification Workflow for Neurodegeneration
Description: Flowchart illustrating data flow from GWAS + multi-omics inputs through knowledge graph integration to ranked target list. Show network proximity concept as sidebar bipartite graph (drug targets on left, AD GWAS genes on right; edge width = proximity score). Final output table with top 10 targets annotated by: druggability tier, genetic evidence level (tier 1/2/3), brain expression (from GTEx), and existing drugs on target. Highlight LRRK2, TREM2, GBA as top-ranked examples. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.
Figure 8. Centralized vs. Federated Learning for Multi-Institutional Drug Discovery
Description: Side-by-side comparison. Left panel (centralized): multiple pharma/hospital icons with arrows flowing to central database server, then to unified model. Right panel (federated): each institution trains local model on local data; only gradient updates (depicted as encrypted packets) flow to central aggregator; global model broadcasted back. Annotate DP noise injection, secure aggregation, and round-based communication protocol. Use padlock icons to emphasize data privacy. Format: TIFF, 300 DPI.

Output: P1. Gilmer, J
Citation: Gilmer, J., et al. 2017. 'Neural Message Passing for Quantum Chemistry.' ICML PMLR:1263–1272
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Unified 8 GNN approaches under MPNN framework; near-DFT accuracy on QM9 for 11 quantum chemical properties. Canonical reference defining message passing in molecular graphs.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary GNN citation; cite as the framework paper unifying message passing methodology.

P2. Yang, K
Citation: Yang, K., et al. 2019. 'Analyzing Learned Molecular Representations for Property Prediction.' JCIM 59(8):3370–3388. DOI:10.1021/acs.jcim.9b00237
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Introduces D-MPNN (Chemprop) — directed bond-level message passing; SOTA on 19 datasets; extensive ablation studies on molecular features and stereochemistry handling.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary Chemprop/D-MPNN citation; cite for ADMET benchmark results.

P3. Xiong, Z
Citation: Xiong, Z., et al. 2020. 'Pushing the Boundaries of Molecular Representation with Graph Attention Mechanism.' J Med Chem 63(16):8749–8760. DOI:10.1021/acs.jmedchem.9b00959
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AttentiveFP integrates graph attention into MPNN; produces atom-level attention maps; outperformed baseline GNNs on 6/8 MoleculeNet tasks. Built-in interpretability via attentionn visualization.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for interpretable GNN; use attention maps as built-in XAI example.

P4. Nguyen, T
Citation: Nguyen, T., et al. 2021. 'GraphDTA: Predicting Drug-Target Binding Affinity with GNNs.' Bioinformatics 37(8):1140–1147. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btaa921
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Drug molecule as graph (GNN encoder) + protein sequence as 1D CNN; outperforms DeepDTA on Davis and KIBA binding affinity benchmarks.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Key DTI prediction citation; cite for drug-target interaction applications of GNNs.

P5. Lin, X
Citation: Lin, X., et al. 2020. 'KGNN: Knowledge Graph Neural Network for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction.' IJCAI 2020:2739–2745
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  KGNN aggregates entity information from pharmacological knowledge graph via attention; AUC 0.964 on DrugBank DDI dataset; outperforms prior methods significantly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Knowledge graph GNN citation for DDI prediction; supports network pharmacology discussion.

P6. Kearnes, S
Citation: Kearnes, S., et al. 2016. 'Molecular Graph Convolutions: Moving Beyond Fingerprints.' J Comput Aid Mol Des 30(8):595–608. DOI:10.1007/s10822-016-9938-8
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  One of the first papers replacing hand-crafted fingerprints with graph convolutions; introduced weave layers processing both atom and bond features jointly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical GNN citation preceding MPNN; traces lineage of molecular GNN development.

P7. Hu, W
Citation: Hu, W., et al. 2020. 'Open Graph Benchmark: Datasets for Machine Learning on Graphs.' NeurIPS 33
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  OGB introduces ogb-molhiv and ogb-molpcba — large-scale molecular graph benchmarks with rigorous scaffold splitting. Standard for GNN evaluation in molecular ML.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for benchmark standardization discussion; reproducible GNN evaluation standard.

P8. Wieder, O
Citation: Wieder, O., et al. 2020. 'A Compact Review of Molecular Property Prediction with GNNs.' Drug Discovery Today: Technologies 37:1–12. DOI:10.1016/j.ddtec.2020.11.009
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Comprehensive review of GNN architectures for molecular property prediction; covers graph convolutions, attention, directed messages, 3D-aware GNNs; practical architecture selecttion guidance.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Use as review citation for GNN overview; complements primary research papers.

Folder 04: Generative AI for Molecular Design
Chapter Relevance: De novo drug design — Section 7 core content

P1. Gomez-Bombarelli, R
Citation: Gomez-Bombarelli, R., et al. 2018. 'Automatic Chemical Design Using a Data-Driven Continuous Representation.' ACS Central Science 4(2):268–276. DOI:10.1021/acscentsci.7b00572
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First VAE on SMILES for continuous molecular optimization; Bayesian optimization in latent space navigates toward desired drug-like properties. Created the paradigm for all subseequent latent space molecular design.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational generative AI citation; cite as origin of continuous molecular latent space optimization.

P2. Jin, W
Citation: Jin, W., R. Barzilay, and T. Jaakkola. 2018. 'Junction Tree Variational Autoencoder for Molecular Graph Generation.' ICML PMLR:2323–2332
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  JT-VAE decomposes molecules into junction trees (scaffolds + side chains); 97.2% reconstruction accuracy; scaffold-constrained optimization. Significantly outperformed SMILES-VAEE in validity.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for VAE subsection; JT-VAE is primary benchmark for graph-based VAE performance.

P3. De Cao, N
Citation: De Cao, N., and T. Kipf. 2018. 'MolGAN: An Implicit Generative Model for Small Molecular Graphs.' arXiv:1805.11973
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Combines GAN with graph convolutions and RL reward to generate molecular adjacency matrices directly; optimizes QED and other properties simultaneously; millisecond inference.   
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for GAN molecular design; MolGAN as representative graph-based GAN.

P4. Olivecrona, M
Citation: Olivecrona, M., et al. 2017. 'Molecular De Novo Design through Deep Reinforcement Learning.' J Cheminform 9(1):48. DOI:10.1186/s13321-017-0235-x
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  REINVENT — LSTM agent with policy gradient RL maximizing composite reward (bioactivity, ADMET, novelty); became industry-standard generative framework at AstraZeneca.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary RL-based generative model citation; industry deployment reference.

P5. Corso, G
Citation: Corso, G., et al. 2022. 'DiffDock: Diffusion Steps, Twists, and Turns for Molecular Docking.' arXiv:2210.01776
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reframes molecular docking as generative modeling via diffusion over ligand poses; 38% top-1 success on blind docking benchmark vs 20% for Glide. Combines translation, rotation,  and torsion diffusion.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite as seminal diffusion model in drug discovery; covers both docking and generative design.

P6. Hoogeboom, E
Citation: Hoogeboom, E., et al. 2022. 'Equivariant Diffusion for Molecule Generation in 3D.' ICML PMLR:8867–8887
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  EDM — SE(3)-equivariant diffusion generating realistic 3D molecular structures respecting rotational/translational symmetry. No post-processing required; correct geometry inherenntly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for 3D generative models; supports pocket-conditioned design discussion.

P7. Blaschke, T
Citation: Blaschke, T., et al. 2020. 'REINVENT 2.0: An AI Tool for De Novo Drug Design.' JCIM 60(12):5918–5922. DOI:10.1021/acs.jcim.0c00915
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  REINVENT 2.0 update with scaffold decoration, linker design, improved transfer learning; widely deployed in pharmaceutical lead optimization campaigns globally.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for industry application of generative RL; supports real-world deployment narrative.

P8. Luo, S
Citation: Luo, S., et al. 2021. 'A 3D Generative Model for Structure-Based Drug Design.' NeurIPS 34:6229–6239
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First 3D generative model conditioned on protein binding pockets; autoregressively places atoms guided by pocket geometry. Historical bridge to TargetDiff and DiffSBDD.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for structure-based generative design evolution toward diffusion models.

Folder 05: NLP & Biomedical Text Mining
Chapter Relevance: Knowledge extraction from literature — Section 6

P1. Lee, J
Citation: Lee, J., et al. 2020. 'BioBERT: A Pre-trained Biomedical Language Representation Model.' Bioinformatics 36(4):1234–1240. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btz682
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Fine-tuned BERT on PubMed + PMC (18.5B tokens); outperforms general BERT on 6 biomedical NLP benchmarks (NER, RE, QA). First domain-adapted BERT for biomedical text.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary BioBERT citation; foundational reference for biomedical language models.

P2. Gu, Y
Citation: Gu, Y., et al. 2021. 'Domain-Specific Language Model Pretraining for Biomedical NLP.' ACM Trans on Computing for Healthcare 3(1):1–23. DOI:10.1145/3458754
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  PubMedBERT — pre-trained from scratch on PubMed only; outperforms BioBERT on 9/11 BLURB benchmarks; demonstrates pure domain pretraining superiority over fine-tuning.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside BioBERT; supports domain-specific pretraining strategy.

P3. Beltagy, I
Citation: Beltagy, I., K. Lo, and A. Cohan. 2019. 'SciBERT: A Pretrained Language Model for Scientific Text.' EMNLP 2019:3615–3620. DOI:10.18653/v1/D19-1371
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Pre-trained on 1.14M Semantic Scholar papers; scientific vocabulary (scivocab); SOTA on scientific NLP tasks; useful for cross-disciplinary drug discovery text mining.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for scientific NLP; cross-disciplinary chemistry + biology literature mining.

✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary Chemprop/D-MPNN citation; cite for ADMET benchmark results.

P3. Xiong, Z
Citation: Xiong, Z., et al. 2020. 'Pushing the Boundaries of Molecular Representation with Graph Attention Mechanism.' J Med Chem 63(16):8749–8760. DOI:10.1021/acs.jmedchem.9b00959
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AttentiveFP integrates graph attention into MPNN; produces atom-level attention maps; outperformed baseline GNNs on 6/8 MoleculeNet tasks. Built-in interpretability via attentionn visualization.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for interpretable GNN; use attention maps as built-in XAI example.

P4. Nguyen, T
Citation: Nguyen, T., et al. 2021. 'GraphDTA: Predicting Drug-Target Binding Affinity with GNNs.' Bioinformatics 37(8):1140–1147. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btaa921
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Drug molecule as graph (GNN encoder) + protein sequence as 1D CNN; outperforms DeepDTA on Davis and KIBA binding affinity benchmarks.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Key DTI prediction citation; cite for drug-target interaction applications of GNNs.

P5. Lin, X
Citation: Lin, X., et al. 2020. 'KGNN: Knowledge Graph Neural Network for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction.' IJCAI 2020:2739–2745
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  KGNN aggregates entity information from pharmacological knowledge graph via attention; AUC 0.964 on DrugBank DDI dataset; outperforms prior methods significantly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Knowledge graph GNN citation for DDI prediction; supports network pharmacology discussion.

P6. Kearnes, S
Citation: Kearnes, S., et al. 2016. 'Molecular Graph Convolutions: Moving Beyond Fingerprints.' J Comput Aid Mol Des 30(8):595–608. DOI:10.1007/s10822-016-9938-8
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  One of the first papers replacing hand-crafted fingerprints with graph convolutions; introduced weave layers processing both atom and bond features jointly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical GNN citation preceding MPNN; traces lineage of molecular GNN development.

P7. Hu, W
Citation: Hu, W., et al. 2020. 'Open Graph Benchmark: Datasets for Machine Learning on Graphs.' NeurIPS 33
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  OGB introduces ogb-molhiv and ogb-molpcba — large-scale molecular graph benchmarks with rigorous scaffold splitting. Standard for GNN evaluation in molecular ML.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for benchmark standardization discussion; reproducible GNN evaluation standard.

P8. Wieder, O
Citation: Wieder, O., et al. 2020. 'A Compact Review of Molecular Property Prediction with GNNs.' Drug Discovery Today: Technologies 37:1–12. DOI:10.1016/j.ddtec.2020.11.009
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Comprehensive review of GNN architectures for molecular property prediction; covers graph convolutions, attention, directed messages, 3D-aware GNNs; practical architecture selecttion guidance.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Use as review citation for GNN overview; complements primary research papers.

Folder 04: Generative AI for Molecular Design
Chapter Relevance: De novo drug design — Section 7 core content

P1. Gomez-Bombarelli, R
Citation: Gomez-Bombarelli, R., et al. 2018. 'Automatic Chemical Design Using a Data-Driven Continuous Representation.' ACS Central Science 4(2):268–276. DOI:10.1021/acscentsci.7b00572
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First VAE on SMILES for continuous molecular optimization; Bayesian optimization in latent space navigates toward desired drug-like properties. Created the paradigm for all subseequent latent space molecular design.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational generative AI citation; cite as origin of continuous molecular latent space optimization.

P2. Jin, W
Citation: Jin, W., R. Barzilay, and T. Jaakkola. 2018. 'Junction Tree Variational Autoencoder for Molecular Graph Generation.' ICML PMLR:2323–2332
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  JT-VAE decomposes molecules into junction trees (scaffolds + side chains); 97.2% reconstruction accuracy; scaffold-constrained optimization. Significantly outperformed SMILES-VAEE in validity.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for VAE subsection; JT-VAE is primary benchmark for graph-based VAE performance.

P3. De Cao, N
Citation: De Cao, N., and T. Kipf. 2018. 'MolGAN: An Implicit Generative Model for Small Molecular Graphs.' arXiv:1805.11973
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Combines GAN with graph convolutions and RL reward to generate molecular adjacency matrices directly; optimizes QED and other properties simultaneously; millisecond inference.   
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for GAN molecular design; MolGAN as representative graph-based GAN.

P4. Olivecrona, M
Citation: Olivecrona, M., et al. 2017. 'Molecular De Novo Design through Deep Reinforcement Learning.' J Cheminform 9(1):48. DOI:10.1186/s13321-017-0235-x
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  REINVENT — LSTM agent with policy gradient RL maximizing composite reward (bioactivity, ADMET, novelty); became industry-standard generative framework at AstraZeneca.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary RL-based generative model citation; industry deployment reference.

P5. Corso, G
Citation: Corso, G., et al. 2022. 'DiffDock: Diffusion Steps, Twists, and Turns for Molecular Docking.' arXiv:2210.01776
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reframes molecular docking as generative modeling via diffusion over ligand poses; 38% top-1 success on blind docking benchmark vs 20% for Glide. Combines translation, rotation,  and torsion diffusion.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite as seminal diffusion model in drug discovery; covers both docking and generative design.

P6. Hoogeboom, E
Citation: Hoogeboom, E., et al. 2022. 'Equivariant Diffusion for Molecule Generation in 3D.' ICML PMLR:8867–8887
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  EDM — SE(3)-equivariant diffusion generating realistic 3D molecular structures respecting rotational/translational symmetry. No post-processing required; correct geometry inherenntly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for 3D generative models; supports pocket-conditioned design discussion.

P7. Blaschke, T
Citation: Blaschke, T., et al. 2020. 'REINVENT 2.0: An AI Tool for De Novo Drug Design.' JCIM 60(12):5918–5922. DOI:10.1021/acs.jcim.0c00915
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  REINVENT 2.0 update with scaffold decoration, linker design, improved transfer learning; widely deployed in pharmaceutical lead optimization campaigns globally.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for industry application of generative RL; supports real-world deployment narrative.

P8. Luo, S
Citation: Luo, S., et al. 2021. 'A 3D Generative Model for Structure-Based Drug Design.' NeurIPS 34:6229–6239
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First 3D generative model conditioned on protein binding pockets; autoregressively places atoms guided by pocket geometry. Historical bridge to TargetDiff and DiffSBDD.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for structure-based generative design evolution toward diffusion models.

Folder 05: NLP & Biomedical Text Mining
Chapter Relevance: Knowledge extraction from literature — Section 6

P1. Lee, J
Citation: Lee, J., et al. 2020. 'BioBERT: A Pre-trained Biomedical Language Representation Model.' Bioinformatics 36(4):1234–1240. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btz682
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Fine-tuned BERT on PubMed + PMC (18.5B tokens); outperforms general BERT on 6 biomedical NLP benchmarks (NER, RE, QA). First domain-adapted BERT for biomedical text.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary BioBERT citation; foundational reference for biomedical language models.

P2. Gu, Y
Citation: Gu, Y., et al. 2021. 'Domain-Specific Language Model Pretraining for Biomedical NLP.' ACM Trans on Computing for Healthcare 3(1):1–23. DOI:10.1145/3458754
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  PubMedBERT — pre-trained from scratch on PubMed only; outperforms BioBERT on 9/11 BLURB benchmarks; demonstrates pure domain pretraining superiority over fine-tuning.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside BioBERT; supports domain-specific pretraining strategy.

P3. Beltagy, I
Citation: Beltagy, I., K. Lo, and A. Cohan. 2019. 'SciBERT: A Pretrained Language Model for Scientific Text.' EMNLP 2019:3615–3620. DOI:10.18653/v1/D19-1371
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Pre-trained on 1.14M Semantic Scholar papers; scientific vocabulary (scivocab); SOTA on scientific NLP tasks; useful for cross-disciplinary drug discovery text mining.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for scientific NLP; cross-disciplinary chemistry + biology literature mining.

args.command: & "C:\Users\skani\Downloads\AI Drug Discovery\venv\Scripts\python.exe" -c "import docx; doc = docx.Document(r'C:\Users\skani\Downloads\BookChapter_References_Complete\Master_Reference_Dossier.docx'); print('\n'.join([p.text for p in doc.paragraphs[200:300]]))"
args.description: Reading the remaining folders (Multi-omics, Target ID, ADMET, Case Studies) in the Master Dossier.

response.output:
Output: 
P4. Luo, R
Citation: Luo, R., et al. 2022. 'BioGPT: Generative Pre-trained Transformer for Biomedical Text.' Briefings in Bioinformatics 23(6):bbac409. DOI:10.1093/bib/bbac409
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Domain-specific GPT-2 on PubMed; SOTA on biomedical RE, QA, and text generation; outperforms BioBERT on relation extraction and generates coherent biomedical text.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for generative biomedical NLP; drug discovery knowledge synthesis applications.

P5. Sung, S
Citation: Sung, S., D. Pascual, and R. Wattenhofer. 2021. 'ChemBERTa: Large-Scale Self-Supervised Pretraining for Molecular Property Prediction.' arXiv:2010.09885
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Applies RoBERTa pretraining to 10M PubChem SMILES; learns molecular representations without property labels; competitive on MoleculeNet benchmarks using pure SMILES language modeling.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for molecular language models; bridges NLP and cheminformatics.

P6. Devlin, J
Citation: Devlin, J., et al. 2019. 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding.' NAACL 2019:4171–4186
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Original BERT — masked LM + next sentence prediction on BooksCorpus + Wikipedia. Established the transfer learning paradigm for NLP that all biomedical BERT variants build upon. 
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational citation; always cite before any BERT variant.

P7. Peng, Y
Citation: Peng, Y., S. Yan, and Z. Lu. 2019. 'Transfer Learning in Biomedical NLP.' BioNLP 2019:58–65. DOI:10.18653/v1/W19-5006
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Systematic evaluation of BERT and ELMo across 10 biomedical NLP tasks; consistent superiority of BERT over ELMo motivates BERT adoption in drug discovery NLP workflows.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for transfer learning evaluation context; justifies BERT over older NLP approaches.

P8. Wishart, D
Citation: Wishart, D.S., et al. 2018. 'DrugBank 5.0: A Major Update.' Nucleic Acids Research 46(D1):D1074–D1082. DOI:10.1093/nar/gkx1037
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Comprehensive drug-target-interaction database: 14,000+ drugs, 5,000+ protein targets, ADMET data, pharmacogenomics, DDI data. Primary resource for knowledge graph construction aand DTI model training.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite as data source for NLP knowledge graph construction pipelines.

Folder 06: Multi-Omics Data Integration
Chapter Relevance: Systems-level drug discovery — Section 8

P1. Argelaguet, R
Citation: Argelaguet, R., et al. 2018. 'Multi-Omics Factor Analysis.' Molecular Systems Biology 14(6):e8124. DOI:10.15252/msb.20178124
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  MOFA — Bayesian group factor analysis framework for unsupervised multi-omics integration; identifies coordinated latent factors across omics layers. Applied to CLL revealing drugg resistance factors.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary multi-omics integration citation; cite MOFA as go-to linear baseline.

P2. Lopez, R
Citation: Lopez, R., et al. 2018. 'Deep Generative Modeling for Single-Cell Transcriptomics.' Nature Methods 15(12):1053–1058. DOI:10.1038/s41592-018-0229-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  scVI — VAE for single-cell RNA-seq handling batch effects, missing data, and zero inflation; enables clustering, DE analysis, and cross-dataset integration at scale.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for single-cell transcriptomics AI; neuron-type-specific analysis in AD/PD.

P3. Subramanian, A
Citation: Subramanian, A., et al. 2017. 'A Next Generation Connectivity Map: L1000 Platform.' Cell 171(6):1437–1452. DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2017.10.049
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  LINCS L1000 — profiles transcriptional responses to 30,000+ perturbations across cell lines; enables signature-based drug repurposing by matching drug-induced vs disease gene exppression changes.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Key citation for transcriptomic-based repurposing; cite when discussing CMap/LINCS approach.

P4. Mathys, H
Citation: Mathys, H., et al. 2019. 'Single-Cell Transcriptomic Analysis of Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature 570(7761):332–337. DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1195-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First large-scale scRNA-seq of AD human brain; identified disease-associated microglia (DAM), oligodendrocyte, and astrocyte subtypes; revealed cell-type-specific transcriptionall changes preceding symptoms.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Landmark scRNA-seq AD study; supports cell-type-specific target identification discussion.

P5. Picard, M
Citation: Picard, M., et al. 2021. 'Integration Strategies of Multi-Omics Data for ML Analysis.' Computational and Structural Biotechnology Journal 19:3735–3746. DOI:10.1016/j.csbj.2021.06.030
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews early, intermediate, and late integration strategies for multi-omics ML; evaluates concatenation, kernel fusion, graph fusion, and autoencoders; practical dataset-size reecommendations.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Review citation for multi-omics strategy; justifies chosen integration approach.

P6. Fang, R
Citation: Fang, R., et al. 2022. 'A Genetics-Led Approach Defines a Core Network for Inflammatory Signalling in AD.' Nature Genetics 54:306–316. DOI:10.1038/s41588-022-01024-z
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Integrates GWAS, eQTL, scRNA-seq, and PPI data to define core neuroinflammation network in AD; identifies INPP5D, BIN1, PTK2B as network hubs with strong druggability evidence.  
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Exemplar of multi-omics-to-target workflow in neurodegeneration; cite as case study.

P7. Zhu, J
Citation: Zhu, J., et al. 2008. 'Integrating Large-Scale Functional Genomic Data to Dissect the Complexity of Yeast Regulatory Networks.' Nature Genetics 40(7):854–861. DOI:10.1038/ng.167
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Early landmark multi-omics network integration paper; introduced the concept of combining genotypic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data to reconstruct regulatory networks.       
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical citation for multi-omics integration; provides conceptual foundation for modern AI approaches.

P8. Lanckriet, G
Citation: Lanckriet, G.R.G., et al. 2004. 'A Statistical Framework for Genomic Data Fusion.' Bioinformatics 20(16):2626–2635. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/bth294
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Introduced kernel-based data fusion for combining heterogeneous genomic data types; foundational computational framework predating deep learning for multi-omics integration.     
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical methods citation; demonstrates evolution from kernel fusion to deep multi-modal integration.

Folder 07: Target Identification & Validation
Chapter Relevance: Target ID and network pharmacology — Section 9

P1. Barabási, A-L
Citation: Barabási, A-L., N. Gulbahce, and J. Loscalzo. 2011. 'Network Medicine.' Nature Reviews Genetics 12(1):56–68. DOI:10.1038/nrg2918
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Established network medicine framework — diseases cluster in specific interactome modules; drug targets must reside within or proximal to the disease module. Foundation for netwoork-based target ID.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational network medicine citation; cite at start of Target Identification section.

P2. Cheng, F
Citation: Cheng, F., I.A. Kovács, and A-L. Barabási. 2019. 'Network-Based Prediction of Drug Combinations.' Nature Communications 10(1):1197. DOI:10.1038/s41467-019-09186-x
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Network proximity predicts synergistic drug combinations; targets proximal in interactome but in non-overlapping modules show highest synergy. Validated against cancer cell line  responses.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for drug combination network pharmacology; supports multi-target design.

P3. Menche, J
Citation: Menche, J., et al. 2015. 'Uncovering Disease-Disease Relationships through the Human Interactome.' Science 347(6224):1257601. DOI:10.1126/science.1257601
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Mapped 299 diseases onto human interactome; diseases with overlapping network modules share genetic, comorbidity, and drug response patterns. Mechanistic basis for cross-disease  crosstalk.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for network medicine context; supports neurodegeneration comorbidity discussion.

P4. Yildirim, M
Citation: Yildirim, M.A., et al. 2007. 'Drug-Target Network.' Nature Biotechnology 25(10):1119–1126. DOI:10.1038/nbt1338
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First systematic drug-target network topology analysis; showed approved drugs cluster at network periphery; multi-target drugs hit hubs. Established druggability landscape conceppt.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical citation for drug-target network analysis; establishes topological framework.

P5. Hopkins, A
Citation: Hopkins, A.L. 2008. 'Network Pharmacology: The Next Paradigm in Drug Discovery.' Nature Chemical Biology 4(11):682–690. DOI:10.1038/nchembio.118
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Coined 'network pharmacology' — argues systems-level multi-target drug design is superior to single-target paradigm; proposes designing molecules modulating multiple disease netwwork nodes.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite when introducing network pharmacology concept; foundational polypharmacology shift.

P6. Fang, J
Citation: Fang, J., et al. 2022. 'Sildenafil Treatment of Men with Erectile Dysfunction and the Risk of Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature Aging 2:1021–1026. DOI:10.1038/s43587-022-00335-0
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Network proximity predicted sildenafil as AD drug; insurance claims analysis of 1.2M men showed 69% reduced AD incidence in sildenafil users. Premier AI-driven AD repurposing vallidation.

Citation: Luo, R., et al. 2022. 'BioGPT: Generative Pre-trained Transformer for Biomedical Text.' Briefings in Bioinformatics 23(6):bbac409. DOI:10.1093/bib/bbac409
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Domain-specific GPT-2 on PubMed; SOTA on biomedical RE, QA, and text generation; outperforms BioBERT on relation extraction and generates coherent biomedical text.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for generative biomedical NLP; drug discovery knowledge synthesis applications.

P5. Sung, S
Citation: Sung, S., D. Pascual, and R. Wattenhofer. 2021. 'ChemBERTa: Large-Scale Self-Supervised Pretraining for Molecular Property Prediction.' arXiv:2010.09885
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Applies RoBERTa pretraining to 10M PubChem SMILES; learns molecular representations without property labels; competitive on MoleculeNet benchmarks using pure SMILES language modeling.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for molecular language models; bridges NLP and cheminformatics.

P6. Devlin, J
Citation: Devlin, J., et al. 2019. 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding.' NAACL 2019:4171–4186
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Original BERT — masked LM + next sentence prediction on BooksCorpus + Wikipedia. Established the transfer learning paradigm for NLP that all biomedical BERT variants build upon. 
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational citation; always cite before any BERT variant.

P7. Peng, Y
Citation: Peng, Y., S. Yan, and Z. Lu. 2019. 'Transfer Learning in Biomedical NLP.' BioNLP 2019:58–65. DOI:10.18653/v1/W19-5006
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Systematic evaluation of BERT and ELMo across 10 biomedical NLP tasks; consistent superiority of BERT over ELMo motivates BERT adoption in drug discovery NLP workflows.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for transfer learning evaluation context; justifies BERT over older NLP approaches.

P8. Wishart, D
Citation: Wishart, D.S., et al. 2018. 'DrugBank 5.0: A Major Update.' Nucleic Acids Research 46(D1):D1074–D1082. DOI:10.1093/nar/gkx1037
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Comprehensive drug-target-interaction database: 14,000+ drugs, 5,000+ protein targets, ADMET data, pharmacogenomics, DDI data. Primary resource for knowledge graph construction aand DTI model training.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite as data source for NLP knowledge graph construction pipelines.

Folder 06: Multi-Omics Data Integration
Chapter Relevance: Systems-level drug discovery — Section 8

P1. Argelaguet, R
Citation: Argelaguet, R., et al. 2018. 'Multi-Omics Factor Analysis.' Molecular Systems Biology 14(6):e8124. DOI:10.15252/msb.20178124
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  MOFA — Bayesian group factor analysis framework for unsupervised multi-omics integration; identifies coordinated latent factors across omics layers. Applied to CLL revealing drugg resistance factors.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary multi-omics integration citation; cite MOFA as go-to linear baseline.

P2. Lopez, R
Citation: Lopez, R., et al. 2018. 'Deep Generative Modeling for Single-Cell Transcriptomics.' Nature Methods 15(12):1053–1058. DOI:10.1038/s41592-018-0229-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  scVI — VAE for single-cell RNA-seq handling batch effects, missing data, and zero inflation; enables clustering, DE analysis, and cross-dataset integration at scale.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for single-cell transcriptomics AI; neuron-type-specific analysis in AD/PD.

P3. Subramanian, A
Citation: Subramanian, A., et al. 2017. 'A Next Generation Connectivity Map: L1000 Platform.' Cell 171(6):1437–1452. DOI:10.1016/j.cell.2017.10.049
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  LINCS L1000 — profiles transcriptional responses to 30,000+ perturbations across cell lines; enables signature-based drug repurposing by matching drug-induced vs disease gene exppression changes.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Key citation for transcriptomic-based repurposing; cite when discussing CMap/LINCS approach.

P4. Mathys, H
Citation: Mathys, H., et al. 2019. 'Single-Cell Transcriptomic Analysis of Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature 570(7761):332–337. DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1195-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First large-scale scRNA-seq of AD human brain; identified disease-associated microglia (DAM), oligodendrocyte, and astrocyte subtypes; revealed cell-type-specific transcriptionall changes preceding symptoms.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Landmark scRNA-seq AD study; supports cell-type-specific target identification discussion.

P5. Picard, M
Citation: Picard, M., et al. 2021. 'Integration Strategies of Multi-Omics Data for ML Analysis.' Computational and Structural Biotechnology Journal 19:3735–3746. DOI:10.1016/j.csbj.2021.06.030
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews early, intermediate, and late integration strategies for multi-omics ML; evaluates concatenation, kernel fusion, graph fusion, and autoencoders; practical dataset-size reecommendations.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Review citation for multi-omics strategy; justifies chosen integration approach.

P6. Fang, R
Citation: Fang, R., et al. 2022. 'A Genetics-Led Approach Defines a Core Network for Inflammatory Signalling in AD.' Nature Genetics 54:306–316. DOI:10.1038/s41588-022-01024-z
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Integrates GWAS, eQTL, scRNA-seq, and PPI data to define core neuroinflammation network in AD; identifies INPP5D, BIN1, PTK2B as network hubs with strong druggability evidence.  
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Exemplar of multi-omics-to-target workflow in neurodegeneration; cite as case study.

P7. Zhu, J
Citation: Zhu, J., et al. 2008. 'Integrating Large-Scale Functional Genomic Data to Dissect the Complexity of Yeast Regulatory Networks.' Nature Genetics 40(7):854–861. DOI:10.1038/ng.167
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Early landmark multi-omics network integration paper; introduced the concept of combining genotypic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data to reconstruct regulatory networks.       
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical citation for multi-omics integration; provides conceptual foundation for modern AI approaches.

P8. Lanckriet, G
Citation: Lanckriet, G.R.G., et al. 2004. 'A Statistical Framework for Genomic Data Fusion.' Bioinformatics 20(16):2626–2635. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/bth294
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Introduced kernel-based data fusion for combining heterogeneous genomic data types; foundational computational framework predating deep learning for multi-omics integration.     
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical methods citation; demonstrates evolution from kernel fusion to deep multi-modal integration.

Folder 07: Target Identification & Validation
Chapter Relevance: Target ID and network pharmacology — Section 9

P1. Barabási, A-L
Citation: Barabási, A-L., N. Gulbahce, and J. Loscalzo. 2011. 'Network Medicine.' Nature Reviews Genetics 12(1):56–68. DOI:10.1038/nrg2918
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Established network medicine framework — diseases cluster in specific interactome modules; drug targets must reside within or proximal to the disease module. Foundation for netwoork-based target ID.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Foundational network medicine citation; cite at start of Target Identification section.

P2. Cheng, F
Citation: Cheng, F., I.A. Kovács, and A-L. Barabási. 2019. 'Network-Based Prediction of Drug Combinations.' Nature Communications 10(1):1197. DOI:10.1038/s41467-019-09186-x
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Network proximity predicts synergistic drug combinations; targets proximal in interactome but in non-overlapping modules show highest synergy. Validated against cancer cell line  responses.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for drug combination network pharmacology; supports multi-target design.

P3. Menche, J
Citation: Menche, J., et al. 2015. 'Uncovering Disease-Disease Relationships through the Human Interactome.' Science 347(6224):1257601. DOI:10.1126/science.1257601
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Mapped 299 diseases onto human interactome; diseases with overlapping network modules share genetic, comorbidity, and drug response patterns. Mechanistic basis for cross-disease  crosstalk.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for network medicine context; supports neurodegeneration comorbidity discussion.

P4. Yildirim, M
Citation: Yildirim, M.A., et al. 2007. 'Drug-Target Network.' Nature Biotechnology 25(10):1119–1126. DOI:10.1038/nbt1338
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  First systematic drug-target network topology analysis; showed approved drugs cluster at network periphery; multi-target drugs hit hubs. Established druggability landscape conceppt.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical citation for drug-target network analysis; establishes topological framework.

P5. Hopkins, A
Citation: Hopkins, A.L. 2008. 'Network Pharmacology: The Next Paradigm in Drug Discovery.' Nature Chemical Biology 4(11):682–690. DOI:10.1038/nchembio.118
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Coined 'network pharmacology' — argues systems-level multi-target drug design is superior to single-target paradigm; proposes designing molecules modulating multiple disease netwwork nodes.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite when introducing network pharmacology concept; foundational polypharmacology shift.

P6. Fang, J
Citation: Fang, J., et al. 2022. 'Sildenafil Treatment of Men with Erectile Dysfunction and the Risk of Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature Aging 2:1021–1026. DOI:10.1038/s43587-022-00335-0
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Network proximity predicted sildenafil as AD drug; insurance claims analysis of 1.2M men showed 69% reduced AD incidence in sildenafil users. Premier AI-driven AD repurposing vallidation.

P7. Bodenreider, O
Citation: Bodenreider, O. 2004. 'The Unified Medical Language System (UMLS).' Nucleic Acids Research 32(D):D267–D270. DOI:10.1093/nar/gkh061
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  UMLS — 4M+ concepts from 200+ biomedical vocabularies in unified semantic framework. Underpins all biomedical knowledge graph construction and NLP entity normalization.

P8. Sadegh, S
Citation: Sadegh, S., et al. 2021. 'Network Medicine for Disease Module Identification and Drug Repurposing with NeDRex.' Nature Communications 12(1):6848. DOI:10.1038/s41467-021-27138-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  NeDRex — open platform integrating 10 biomedical databases; provides network-based disease module ID, drug repurposing, and target prioritization tools. Applied to COVID-19 and nneurodegeneration.

Folder 08: ADMET Prediction
Chapter Relevance: ADMET and CNS drug design challenge — Sections 3, 4, and Future Challenges

P1. Delaney, J
Citation: Delaney, J.S. 2004. 'ESOL: Estimating Aqueous Solubility Directly from Molecular Structure.' JCICS 44(3):1000–1005. DOI:10.1021/ci034243x
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  ESOL linear regression model predicting aqueous solubility from 4 descriptors (MW, logP, rotatable bonds, aromatic proportion). Classic ADMET prediction benchmark; demonstrates tthat simple ML achieves meaningful accuracy.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Classic ADMET prediction citation; anchor historical discussion of property prediction.

P2. Bickerton, G
Citation: Bickerton, G.R., et al. 2012. 'Quantifying the Chemical Beauty of Drugs.' Nature Chemistry 4(2):90–98. DOI:10.1038/nchem.1243
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  QED — aggregates 8 molecular properties into a single drug-likeness score (0–1). Standard metric for generative model output evaluation in virtually every de novo design paper.  
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for drug-likeness metric; QED is universal evaluation metric for generative design.

P3. Ertl, P
Citation: Ertl, P., and A. Schuffenhauer. 2009. 'Estimation of Synthetic Accessibility Score.' J Cheminform 1(1):8. DOI:10.1186/1758-2946-1-8
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  SA Score — fragment frequency-based scoring of synthetic complexity on 1-10 scale. Used alongside QED to penalize chemically unrealizable generative model outputs.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for synthetic feasibility assessment; essential for generative model evaluation.

P4. Daina, A
Citation: Daina, A., O. Michielin, and V. Zoete. 2017. 'SwissADME: A Free Web Tool to Evaluate Pharmacokinetics.' Scientific Reports 7:42717. DOI:10.1038/srep42717
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  SwissADME — freely accessible ADMET platform covering lipophilicity, solubility, BBB permeability, and 5 drug-likeness rules. One of the most heavily used ADMET screening tools gglobally.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite as practical ADMET screening tool; supports CNS drug design discussion.

P5. Wang, Z
Citation: Wang, Z., et al. 2024. 'ADMET-AI: A Machine Learning ADMET Platform.' Bioinformatics 40(7):btae416. DOI:10.1093/bioinformatics/btae416
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  ADMET-AI — Chemprop-based multitask ADMET platform predicting 41 properties; best-in-class accuracy; deployed as open web server and Python package; supports billion-scale virtuaal screening.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Current SOTA ADMET tool citation; illustrates scalability of deep learning ADMET pipelines.

P6. Muehlbacher, M
Citation: Muehlbacher, M., et al. 2011. 'Qualitative Prediction of Blood-Brain Barrier Permeability through an SVM.' J Comput Aid Mol Des 25(12):1095–1106. DOI:10.1007/s10822-011-9478-1
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  SVM model for BBB permeability classification using 29 molecular descriptors; among first ML-based BBB prediction models; highlights BBB modeling challenge for CNS drugs.        
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for CNS/BBB prediction challenge; motivates specialized CNS ADMET models.

P7. Lipinski, C
Citation: Lipinski, C.A., et al. 2001. 'Experimental and Computational Approaches to Estimate Solubility and Permeability in Drug Discovery.' Adv Drug Delivery Reviews 46(1-3):3–26. DOI:10.1016/S0169-409X(00)00129-0
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Rule of Five (Ro5) — MW≤500, logP≤5, HBD≤5, HBA≤10. Most cited paper in drug discovery; foundational filter underlying all ADMET screening pipelines globally.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Must-cite foundational ADMET paper; cite when introducing drug-likeness and oral bioavailability.

P8. Svetnik, V
Citation: Svetnik, V., et al. 2005. 'Application of Breiman's Random Forest to Modeling Structure-Activity Relationships of Pharmaceutical Molecules.' Multiple Classifier Systems, LNCS 3541:334–343. DOI:10.1007/11494683_35
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Extended application of RF to large-scale ADMET datasets; demonstrated RF robustness, variable importance analysis, and superior performance over traditional QSAR on real pharmacceutical data.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for RF ADMET methodology; bridges general RF theory to pharmaceutical ADMET practice.

Folder 09: Protein Structure Prediction
Chapter Relevance: AlphaFold2 and structural biology — Deep Learning section and target accessibility

P1. Jumper, J
Citation: Jumper, J., et al. 2021. 'Highly Accurate Protein Structure Prediction with AlphaFold.' Nature 596(7873):583–589. DOI:10.1038/s41586-021-03819-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AlphaFold2 achieved median Cα RMSD 0.96Å on CASP14 — essentially experimental accuracy. Evoformer Transformer + structure module predicts 3D coordinates directly. 200M+ structurees released publicly.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Central AI breakthrough citation; arguably most impactful single AI paper in biology; cite in DL and target ID sections.

P2. Baek, M
Citation: Baek, M., et al. 2021. 'Accurate Prediction of Protein Structures and Interactions Using a Three-Track Neural Network.' Science 373(6557):871–876. DOI:10.1126/science.abj8754
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  RoseTTAFold — three-track attention architecture processing 1D, 2D, and 3D protein information simultaneously; accurate, faster than AF2, open-source with active community.      
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside AlphaFold2 as complementary open-source structure prediction tool.

P3. Lin, Z
Citation: Lin, Z., et al. 2023. 'Evolutionary-Scale Prediction of Atomic-Level Protein Structure with a Language Model.' Science 379(6637):1123–1130. DOI:10.1126/science.ade2574
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  ESM-2 + ESMFold — 15B parameter protein LM predicts structure without MSA; 60× faster than AF2; enables proteome-scale IDP analysis relevant to tau and alpha-synuclein in neurodeegeneration.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for MSA-free protein LM; especially relevant for disordered neurodegenerative proteins.

P4. Rives, A
Citation: Rives, A., et al. 2021. 'Biological Structure and Function Emerge from Scaling Unsupervised Learning to 250 Million Protein Sequences.' PNAS 118(15):e2016239118. DOI:10.1073/pnas.2016239118    
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  ESM-1b — 250M protein sequence LM; learns structural and functional information without explicit structural supervision. Evolutionary information emerges from large-scale languagge model training.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for protein language model pretraining; supports self-supervised learning discussion.

P5. Watson, J
Citation: Watson, J.L., et al. 2023. 'De Novo Design of Protein Structure and Function with RFdiffusion.' Nature 620(7976):1089–1100. DOI:10.1038/s41586-023-06415-8
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  RFdiffusion — diffusion model for protein backbone generation; creates new proteins with specified function (binders, enzymes); >50% experimental validation success rate for noveel designs.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for protein generative design; parallel to small molecule diffusion models.

P6. Varadi, M
Citation: Varadi, M., et al. 2022. 'AlphaFold Protein Structure Database.' Nucleic Acids Research 50(D1):D439–D444. DOI:10.1093/nar/gkab1061
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AlphaFold DB — >200M predicted structures covering UniProt; freely accessible via API; enables docking and drug design for virtually any protein without experimental structure.  
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside AlphaFold2 paper for database resource; supports undruggable target accessibility.

P7. Senior, A
Citation: Senior, A.W., et al. 2020. 'Improved Protein Structure Prediction Using Potentials from Deep Learning.' Nature 577(7792):706–710. DOI:10.1038/s41586-019-1923-7
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AlphaFold1 — first demonstration that DL potentials substantially improve protein structure prediction; established the ML-structure prediction paradigm culminating in AlphaFold22.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical citation; narrates progression from AlphaFold1 to AlphaFold2.

P8. Craik, D
Citation: Craik, D.J., et al. 2013. 'The Future of Peptide-Based Drugs.' Chemical Biology and Drug Design 81(1):136–147. DOI:10.1111/cbdd.12055
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews peptide drugs — BBB penetration challenges; cyclic and stapled peptides as CNS targeting solutions. Contextualizes structural biology applications in CNS drug design for  neurodegeneration.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Background for peptide-based approaches in neurodegeneration; complements structure-based design.

Folder 10: Neurodegeneration & AI — Disease-Specific
Chapter Relevance: Disease-specific case studies throughout all chapter sections

P1. Jiang, P
Citation: Jiang, P., and A.D. Schäfer. 2021. 'AlzGPS: A Genome-Wide Positioning Systems Platform for Multi-Target Alzheimer's Drug Discovery.' Alzheimer's Research and Therapy 13(1):71. DOI:10.1186/s13195-021-00806-5
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  AlzGPS integrates GWAS, transcriptomics, proteomics, and DTI data into a genome-wide positioning system for AD; identifies 103 druggable genes with multi-omics evidence. Disease--specific AI platform.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Disease-specific platform citation; example of integrated AI platform for AD target identification.

P2. Nalls, M
Citation: Nalls, M.A., et al. 2019. 'Identification of Novel Risk Loci, Causal Insights, and Heritable Risk for Parkinson's Disease: A Meta-GWAS.' Lancet Neurology 18(12):1091–1102. DOI:10.1016/S1474-4422(19)30320-5
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Largest PD GWAS: 90 independent risk variants; genetic evidence for LRRK2, GBA, SNCA, and lysosomal pathway. Foundation for AI-driven PD target prioritization.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary PD genetics citation; cite as genetic evidence base for AI target ID in PD.

P3. Lambert, J-C
Citation: Lambert, J-C., et al. 2013. 'Meta-Analysis of 74,046 Individuals Identifies 11 New Susceptibility Loci for Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature Genetics 45(12):1452–1458. DOI:10.1038/ng.2802
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Large IGAP AD GWAS; identified BIN1, CLU, PICALM, CR1. Generated genetic target space that AI prioritization tools are trained and validated on.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary AD genetics citation; cite for GWAS-to-target workflows.

P4. Kunkle, B
Citation: Kunkle, B.W., et al. 2019. 'Genetic Meta-Analysis of Diagnosed Alzheimer's Disease.' Nature Genetics 51(3):414–430. DOI:10.1038/s41588-019-0358-2
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  IGAP2 meta-GWAS; 5 new AD loci (IQCK, ACE, ADAM10); implicated lipid processing, innate immunity, synaptic function; multi-ancestry genetic evidence for network-based target disccovery.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside Lambert 2013 for comprehensive AD genetic landscape.

P5. Hampel, H
Citation: Hampel, H., et al. 2022. 'The Alzheimer Precision Medicine Initiative (APMI).' J Alzheimer's Disease 52(4):1127–1140. DOI:10.3233/JAD-160374
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Proposes precision medicine framework for AD using AI to stratify patients by molecular subtype; advocates for biomarker-driven clinical trial design integrating multi-omics.    
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for precision medicine/patient stratification context in neurodegeneration.

P6. Hinton, G
Citation: Hinton, G., et al. 2018. 'Deep Learning — A Technology with the Potential to Transform Health Care.' JAMA 320(11):1101–1102. DOI:10.1001/jama.2018.11100
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Expert perspective on DL transforming radiology, pathology, and genomic medicine; discusses regulatory and validation challenges; highly cited clinical AI perspective.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Clinical AI perspective citation; bridges computational methods to clinical deployment.

P7. Fang, J
Citation: Fang, J., et al. 2023. 'Endophenotype-Based In Silico Network Medicine Discovery Combined with Insurance Record Data Mining Identifies Sildenafil as a Candidate Drug for AD.' Nature Aging 3:1–14.
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Expanded sildenafil AD analysis; used endophenotype network proximity + large-scale EHR data; identified additional PDE5 inhibitors with AD protective signals; demonstrates AI-too-EHR pipeline.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Extended repurposing case study; cite in both target identification and drug repurposing sections.

P8. Villegas-Llerena, C
Citation: Villegas-Llerena, C., et al. 2016. 'Microglial Genes Regulating Neuroinflammation in the Progression of AD.' Current Opinion in Neurobiology 36:74–81. DOI:10.1016/j.conb.2015.10.004
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews TREM2, CD33, CR1, and other microglial genetic risk factors for AD; mechanistic basis for neuroinflammation as druggable target class; motivates AI-based neuroinflammatioon target prioritization.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Background citation for neuroinflammation target class in AD; supports TREM2/INPP5D discussion.

Folder 11: Explainable AI & Federated Learning
Chapter Relevance: Model transparency and privacy — Section 10

P1. McMahan, H
Citation: McMahan, H.B., et al. 2017. 'Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data.' AISTATS:1273–1282
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Foundational FL paper — introduces FedAvg; demonstrates local gradient computation + global aggregation trains competitive models without data sharing. Defines the FL paradigm.  
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Must-cite foundational FL reference; cite at start of federated learning section.

P2. Rieke, N
Citation: Rieke, N., et al. 2020. 'The Future of Digital Health with Federated Learning.' npj Digital Medicine 3(1):119. DOI:10.1038/s41746-020-00323-1
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews FL applications in clinical imaging, EHR, and genomics; discusses non-IID data challenges, communication costs, Byzantine robustness; advocates FL as default multi-centerr architecture.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for clinical FL application review; bridges FL methodology to healthcare deployment.

P3. Lundberg, S
Citation: Lundberg, S.M., and S-I. Lee. 2017. 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions.' NeurIPS 30:4765–4774
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  SHAP — game-theoretic attribution using Shapley values; unifies LIME, DeepLIFT, LASSO. TreeSHAP is exact for tree models; DeepSHAP approximates for DNNs. Most widely used XAI metthod.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary XAI citation; cite for feature attribution in molecular property prediction.

P4. Ribeiro, M
Citation: Ribeiro, M.T., S. Singh, and C. Guestrin. 2016. 'Why Should I Trust You? Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier.' KDD:1135–1144. DOI:10.1145/2939672.2939778
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  LIME — perturbs input to generate locally linear explanations of any black-box model; widely applied to molecular and clinical ML for interpretable feature importance.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite alongside SHAP for local explanation methods; model-agnostic XAI perspective.

P5. Jimenez-Luna, J
Citation: Jimenez-Luna, J., F. Grisoni, and G. Schneider. 2020. 'Drug Discovery with Explainable AI.' Nature Machine Intelligence 2(10):573–584. DOI:10.1038/s42256-020-00236-4
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Comprehensive XAI review in drug discovery; covers saliency maps, attention, SHAP, LIME, concept-based explanations; evaluates fidelity and stability; framework for regulatory-grrade transparency.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary XAI drug discovery review; comprehensive reference for XAI subsection.

P6. Selvaraju, R
Citation: Selvaraju, R.R., et al. 2017. 'Grad-CAM: Visual Explanations from Deep Networks via Gradient-Based Localization.' ICCV:618–626. DOI:10.1109/ICCV.2017.74
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Grad-CAM uses final convolutional layer gradients to produce localization heat maps. Applied to protein structures and phenotypic screening images to identify pharmacologically rrelevant regions.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for image-based XAI; CNN applications in cryo-EM and cell painting workflows.

P7. Warnat-Herresthal, S
Citation: Warnat-Herresthal, S., et al. 2021. 'Swarm Learning for Decentralized and Confidential Clinical Machine Learning.' Nature 594(7862):265–270. DOI:10.1038/s41586-021-03583-3
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Swarm learning — blockchain-secured FL without central server; applied to leukemia, COVID, and TB diagnosis from blood transcriptomics; each node contributes equally.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for advanced FL architecture; decentralized privacy-first clinical AI discussion.

P8. Doshi-Velez, F
Citation: Doshi-Velez, F., and B. Kim. 2017. 'Towards a Rigorous Science of Interpretable Machine Learning.' arXiv:1702.08608
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Formal taxonomy of interpretability and evaluation metrics; application-grounded, human-grounded, and functionally-grounded evaluation framework; foundational for rigorous XAI asssessment.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Theoretical XAI citation; cite for criteria for regulatory-grade model interpretability.

Folder 12: Drug Repurposing (Computational)
Chapter Relevance: Computational repurposing strategies — Section 9

P1. Pushpakom, S
Citation: Pushpakom, S., et al. 2019. 'Drug Repurposing: Progress, Challenges and Recommendations.' Nature Reviews Drug Discovery 18(1):41–58. DOI:10.1038/nrd.2018.168
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Definitive review of computational/experimental drug repurposing; covers serendipitous, hypothesis-driven, and computational approaches; discusses validation frameworks and regullatory fast-track pathways.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Primary drug repurposing review citation; cite at start of repurposing discussion.

P2. Zhou, Y
Citation: Zhou, Y., et al. 2020. 'Artificial Intelligence in COVID-19 Drug Repurposing.' The Lancet Digital Health 2(12):e667–e676. DOI:10.1016/S2589-7500(20)30192-8
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  Reviews AI approaches to COVID-19 repurposing; identifies dexamethasone, baricitinib predictions validated in clinical trials. Demonstrates rapid AI-to-clinic pipeline as proof-oof-principle.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Cite for AI repurposing validation; COVID-19 provides proof-of-principle for neurodegeneration timelines.

P3. Gottlieb, A
Citation: Gottlieb, A., et al. 2011. 'PREDICT: A Method for Inferring Novel Drug Indications.' Molecular Systems Biology 7(1):496. DOI:10.1038/msb.2011.26
📋 Key Findings / Gist:  PREDICT — combines drug-drug, disease-disease, and drug-disease similarity networks for indication prediction; 50% precision on novel indications; applied to rare diseases and peersonalized medicine.
✏️  How to Incorporate:  Historical computational repurposing citation; cite alongside more recent DL methods.

P4. Gysi, D

P5. Menden, M

P7. Iorio, F

How to Use This Reference Dossier
Step 1: Download the Papers
For each paper listed, search Google Scholar, PubMed, or Sci-Hub using the DOI provided. Save the PDF into the corresponding numbered subfolder on your computer (e.g., save Rogers & Hahn 2010 into folder '01_Classical_ML_QSAR'). Name each file using the format: [AuthorLastName_Year_KeyWord.pdf] — for example: Rogers_2010_ECFP.pdf.
Step 2: Read Using the Gist as Guide
Before reading each paper in full, read the 'Key Findings / Gist' entry in this dossier. This orients you to what to focus on. As you read, highlight sections that directly relate to the 'How to Incorporate' instruction. Add your own handwritten notes in the margin or use annotation software (Adobe Acrobat, Zotero, Mendeley).
Step 3: Rewrite in Your Own Voice
IMPORTANT: The book chapter has a 0% AI-generated text requirement. Use the content in this dossier as structured notes and skeleton points only. Every sentence that goes into your chapter must be written entirely in your own words, based on your understanding of the papers you have read. Do not copy any sentence directly from this dossier or from the original papers.
Step 4: Cite Using the HOW TO Incorporate Field
Each paper entry specifies exactly where in the chapter it should be cited and what claim it supports. Follow this guide during writing to ensure every claim in your chapter has a matching reference. Chicago Author-Date style: use (Author Year) in-text and list full citations in the References section.
Step 5: Check Plagiarism and AI Detection
Before submission, run the final manuscript through: (1) iThenticate or Turnitin for plagiarism, (2) ZeroGPT or GPTZero for AI detection. Aim for <10% similarity on plagiarism and 0% AI-generated content flags. Rephrase any flagged passages.
Recommended Reading Order for Beginners
If you are new to the AI drug discovery field, we recommend reading the papers in this order: Start with Lipinski 2001 (Ro5) → Rogers 2010 (ECFP) → Breiman 2001 (RF) → Chen 2018 (DL Review) → Gilmer 2017 (MPNN) → Jumper 2021 (AlphaFold2) → Barabási 2011 (Network Medicine) → Pushpakom 2019 (Repurposing Review). This sequence builds a coherent conceptual foundation before diving into specialist topics.

How to Use This Reference Dossier

For each paper listed, search Google Scholar, PubMed, or Sci-Hub using the DOI provided. Save the PDF into the corresponding numbered subfolder on your computer (e.g., save Rogers & Hahn 2010 into folder
'01_Classical_ML_QSAR'). Name each file using the format: [AuthorLastName_Year_KeyWord.pdf] — for example: Rogers_2010_ECFP.pdf.
Step 2: Read Using the Gist as Guide

Before reading each paper in full, read the 'Key Findings / Gist' entry in this dossier. This orients you to what to focus on. As you read, highlight sections that directly relate to the 'How to Incorpor
ate' instruction. Add your own handwritten notes in the margin or use annotation software (Adobe Acrobat, Zotero, Mendeley).
Step 3: Rewrite in Your Own Voice

IMPORTANT: The book chapter has a 0% AI-generated text requirement. Use the content in this dossier as structured notes and skeleton points only. Every sentence that goes into your chapter must be writte
n entirely in your own words, based on your understanding of the papers you have read. Do not copy any sentence directly from this dossier or from the original papers.
Step 4: Cite Using the HOW TO Incorporate Field

Each paper entry specifies exactly where in the chapter it should be cited and what claim it supports. Follow this guide during writing to ensure every claim in your chapter has a matching reference. Chi
cago Author-Date style: use (Author Year) in-text and list full citations in the References section.
Step 5: Check Plagiarism and AI Detection

Before submission, run the final manuscript through: (1) iThenticate or Turnitin for plagiarism, (2) ZeroGPT or GPTZero for AI detection. Aim for <10% similarity on plagiarism and 0% AI-generated content

If you are new to the AI drug discovery field, we recommend reading the papers in this order: Start with Lipinski 2001 (Ro5) → Rogers 2010 (ECFP) → Breiman 2001 (RF) → Chen 2018 (DL Review) → Gilmer 2017
(MPNN) → Jumper 2021 (AlphaFold2) → Barabási 2011 (Network Medicine) → Pushpakom 2019 (Repurposing Review). This sequence builds a coherent conceptual foundation before diving into specialist topics.
